topics.title: Chart Drive Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML508 - CLINICAL INSTRUMENTATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Chart Drive Circuit ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Chart Drive Circuit (CDC):** A precision electromechanical subsystem used in clinical recorders (ECG, EEG, EMG, phonocardiograph, polygraph) that translates the steady time-base of a physiological signal into a uniform, calibrated linear motion of the recording medium (heat-sensitive, pressure-sensitive, or ink-jet paper) past a stationary or oscillating stylus/thermal array. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Chart Drive Circuit is **not merely a motor-driving circuit**. It is a complete **closed-loop velocity control system** that guarantees the paper advances at an internationally standardized, calibrated speed — independent of load variations, mains fluctuations, motor wear, or temperature drift. | Clinical Recorder | **Standard Chart Speed (v)** | Calibration Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | ECG (Electrocardiograph) | **25 mm/s** | 1 small box (1 mm) = **0.04 s**; 1 large box (5 mm) = **0.20 s** | | EEG (Electroencephalograph) | **30 mm/s** (also 15, 60 mm/s selectable) | Used for time-locked cortical wave analysis | | EMG (Electromyograph) | **10 – 200 mm/s** (variable) | Motor unit action potential sweep | | Phonocardiograph / PCG | **50 – 100 mm/s** | Heart sound – murmur timing | | Defibrillator Strip | **25 mm/s** | Post-shock rhythm documentation | > [!NOTE] > **Why 25 mm/s for ECG?** This is a global standard mandated by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC 60601-2-25). A heart rate of 60 bpm → 1 beat per second → 25 mm between consecutive R-waves on paper, allowing instant visual assessment of rate, intervals, and morphology. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient Pedometer" Imagine an old grandfather clock. The pendulum swings with absolute regularity — **once per second** — and drives the minute hand at a perfectly constant angular velocity, *no matter how much friction the gears suffer*. The Chart Drive Circuit performs the **exact same role** in a medical recorder: - **Pendulum** → Reference clock oscillator (quartz crystal or 555 astable) - **Pendulum escapement** → Feedback comparator / Phase-Locked Loop - **Gear train** → Speed reduction gearbox - **Minute hand** → Chart paper roller (sprocket wheel) - **Clock face** → Thermal paper strip Just as a clock tells accurate time because its **reference is decoupled from the load**, a chart drive circuit produces accurate time-traces because the **paper velocity is decoupled from motor torque variations** by a feedback control loop. > [!TIP] > **First-Read Intuition:** Think of the chart drive as the *"metronome of medicine."* It does not record the signal itself; it merely provides the **uniform time axis** upon which the signal is drawn. Without it, an ECG would be a meaningless squiggle — with it, a 1 mm box is unambiguously **0.04 seconds of cardiac electrical activity**. --- ### 1.3 Key Performance Specifications (Highlighted) A KTU-grade chart drive circuit must satisfy the following non-negotiable specifications: 1. **Speed Accuracy:** $\pm 2\%$ of nominal (e.g., 25 mm/s $\pm$ 0.5 mm/s) — required for valid interval measurement. 2. **Speed Stability (Wow & Flutter):** $< 1\%$ peak-to-peak over full paper roll. 3. **Starting Torque:** Sufficient to overcome static friction of paper, platen, and stylus head within 50 ms. 4. **Settling Time:** Speed must reach steady-state within 0.5 s of power-on. 5. **Direction Reversal:** Capability to drive paper forward (normal) and reverse (review/zoom) in modern digital recorders. > [!WARNING] > **Calibration Reality Check:** In KTU lab evaluation, a 5% deviation in paper speed *will* cause the examiner to mark the experiment down, because it directly falsifies the patient's clinical time-domain data — a real-world medico-legal issue. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Chart Drive Circuit A modern chart drive system is composed of **four functional blocks** operating in a closed servo loop: #### Block A — Reference Frequency Generator - Produces a **highly stable clock** that defines the *ideal* paper speed. - Implementations: 555 astable multivibrator, quartz crystal oscillator + divider, or microcontroller timer. - Output: a square wave of frequency $f_{ref}$ (typically 1 kHz – 1 MHz, divided down to motor-control range). #### Block B — Error Detector / Comparator - Compares the reference frequency against a **tachogenerator / optical encoder feedback** signal. - Output: an error voltage $V_e = k (f_{ref} - f_{fb})$ proportional to speed deviation. - Advanced systems use a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** with motor tachogenerator as VCO. #### Block C — Power Driver Stage - Boosts the low-power error signal to a current/voltage level capable of driving the DC motor. - Common devices: **BJT Darlington (TIP31, BD139)**, **MOSFET (IRF540)**, or **H-bridge IC (L293D, L298)**. - Modulation: Linear (Class A) or **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** for efficiency. #### Block D — Actuator and Mechanical Linkage - **DC Motor:** Permanent-magnet, low-voltage (6 V – 24 V), low-power (1 W – 5 W). - **Gearbox:** Spur gear reduction with ratio $G \in [10:1,\ 100:1]$ to convert high motor RPM to slow, torque-rich roller RPM. - **Sprocket Wheel / Pinch Roller:** Engages perforations on chart paper, providing **non-slip drive**. - **Tachogenerator / Optical Encoder:** Mounted on motor shaft, generates frequency $f_{fb}$ proportional to actual motor RPM. --- ### 2.2 Operating Principle — Closed-Loop Speed Regulation The system works on the **negative-feedback control principle**: 1. The **reference oscillator** sets the desired paper velocity $v^*$. 2. The **DC motor** spins at speed $n_m$ rpm, geared down to roller speed $n_r = n_m / G$. 3. The **tachogenerator** produces a feedback frequency $f_{fb} = k_t \cdot n_m$ proportional to actual motor speed. 4. The **comparator** produces an error: $f_{err} = f_{ref} - f_{fb}$. 5. The **PWM/driver** increases motor voltage if $f_{err} > 0$ (paper too slow), decreases if $f_{err} < 0$ (paper too fast). 6. At steady state: $f_{fb} \to f_{ref}$, hence $n_m$ (and therefore $v$) becomes **locked to the reference**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** The motor is *not* asked to spin at a fixed voltage. It is *commanded* to spin at a fixed *frequency* derived from the reference. Load disturbances (paper drag, stylus friction) momentarily change $f_{fb}$, the comparator immediately corrects, and the **user-observable chart speed remains constant**. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations. KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems in Clinical Instrumentation Lab are dominated by these four formulae.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Use | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $v = f_{fb} \cdot d_{pulse}$ | Chart speed = feedback frequency × linear distance per encoder pulse | Convert encoder Hz to mm/s | | 2 | $n_r = \dfrac{n_m}{G} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{fb}}{G \cdot p}$ | Roller rpm from motor frequency and gear ratio (p = encoder pulses/rev) | Back-calculate motor spec | | 3 | $v = \pi \cdot D_r \cdot n_r \cdot \dfrac{1}{60}$ | Linear chart speed from roller diameter and roller rpm | Mechanical design of roller | | 4 | $T = 1.44 \,\big/\, [(R_1 + 2R_2) C]$ | 555 Timer astable frequency (the de-facto KTU exam formula) | Design of reference oscillator | | 5 | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\,\%$ | PWM duty cycle percentage | Motor power / speed control | | 6 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \phi}$ | DC motor no-load speed equation | Motor selection analysis | | 7 | $e = \dfrac{n_{actual} - n_{desired}}{n_{desired}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage speed error (calibration check) | Lab viva & record evaluation | > [!TIP] > **Units Vigilance:** Always keep $v$ in **mm/s**, $f$ in **Hz**, and roller diameter $D_r$ in **mm** to match KTU record-book conventions. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Chart drive technology is the unsung backbone of **every time-based diagnostic recorder** ever built. The same closed-loop principles taught here underpin: - **Surgical thermal array recorders** in ICU monitors and anesthesia workstations. - **Fetal heart-rate strip-chart monitors** (cardiotocograph) in labor wards. - **Ambulatory (Holter) recorders** — where a stepper motor replaces DC motor for micro-power sleep-mode operation. - **Modern digital recorders** still require a paper-output option for legal and ICU-beside use; hence this circuit is **not obsolete**. - **Industrial strip-chart recorders** for process variables (temperature, pressure) — the same technology scales to plant control rooms. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Linkage:** Companies like **Philips, GE Healthcare, Nihon Kohden, Mindray, and BPL Medical** use precisely this architecture (with surface-mount upgrades) in their premium ECG machines — a fact examiners love to test. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Chart Speed from Encoder Feedback **Statement:** A chart drive uses a DC motor coupled to a sprocket roller of effective diameter $D_r = 12$ mm through a gear reduction box of ratio $G = 40:1$. An optical encoder on the motor shaft produces **$p = 100$ pulses per revolution**. The motor spins at $n_m = 3000$ rpm. Calculate the **chart speed $v$ in mm/s**. **Solution Walkthrough:** Let the motor shaft produce $f_{fb}$ feedback pulses per second. The pulses-per-revolution of the encoder is $p = 100$. $$ f_{fb} \;=\; \frac{n_m \;\times\; p}{60} $$ Plugging $n_m = 3000$ rpm and $p = 100$: $$ f_{fb} \;=\; \frac{3000 \;\times\; 100}{60} \;=\; \frac{300000}{60} \;=\; 5000~\text{Hz} $$ The roller rotates at the geared-down speed: $$ n_r \;=\; \frac{n_m}{G} \;=\; \frac{3000}{40} \;=\; 75~\text{rpm} $$ The linear distance traveled by the chart per roller revolution equals the roller circumference $\pi D_r$. For $D_r = 12$ mm: $$ \text{Circumference} \;=\; \pi \times 12 \;=\; 12\pi~\text{mm} \;\approx\; 37.699~\text{mm/rev} $$ The chart speed (distance per unit time) is: $$ v \;=\; \frac{n_r \times \pi D_r}{60} \;=\; \frac{75 \times 37.699}{60} $$ $$ v \;=\; \frac{2827.43}{60} \;\approx\; 47.12~\text{mm/s} $$ > **Sanity Check (KTU-style):** ECG standard is 25 mm/s. We got ~47 mm/s, so the gear ratio is too low for ECG. We would need to **raise G to ~80** to reach 25 mm/s. This kind of "design iteration" question is a KTU favourite. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Designing a 555 Timer for Reference Frequency **Statement:** For the chart drive above, we need a **reference clock** at $f_{ref} = 5$ kHz so that the PLL can lock $f_{fb} = 5$ kHz. Design a 555 astable multivibrator with $C = 0.01\ \mu F$. Find $R_1$ and $R_2$. **Solution Walkthrough:** The 555 astable frequency is: $$ f_{ref} \;=\; \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C} $$ Solving for the resistance sum: $$ R_1 + 2R_2 \;=\; \frac{1.44}{f_{ref} \cdot C} \;=\; \frac{1.44}{5000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R_1 + 2R_2 \;=\; \frac{1.44}{5 \times 10^{-8}} \;=\; 2.88 \times 10^{7}~\Omega $$ $$ R_1 + 2R_2 \;\approx\; 28.8~\text{k}\Omega $$ Choosing a **50% duty cycle** condition (KTU's standard simplification: $R_1 \ll R_2$ is neglected, $R_1 = R_2$): $$ R_1 + 2R_2 = R_2 + 2R_2 = 3R_2 \;\Rightarrow\; R_2 = \frac{28.8}{3} = 9.6~\text{k}\Omega $$ $$ R_1 = R_2 = 9.6~\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(use standard value 10 k}\Omega \text{)} $$ The duty cycle is: $$ D \;=\; \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\,\% \;=\; \frac{9.6 + 9.6}{9.6 + 19.2} \times 100\,\% = 66.67\,\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** A 66.67% duty cycle means the motor is energized 2/3 of the time and coasting 1/3. The high time provides driving torque; the low time allows inductive kick-back protection for the driver transistor. This is the *empirically* optimal duty cycle for low-power chart-drive DC motors. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Calibration Percentage Error **Statement:** The KTU lab record requires verification of chart speed. The student measures that the chart paper travels exactly **100 mm in 3.9 seconds** at the "25 mm/s" setting. Calculate the **percentage speed error** and comment on whether the device passes KTU calibration. **Solution Walkthrough:** Measured speed: $$ v_{measured} \;=\; \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} \;=\; \frac{100}{3.9} \;\approx\; 25.641~\text{mm/s} $$ Percentage error (using KTU sign convention — positive means *fast*): $$ e\,\% \;=\; \frac{v_{measured} - v_{nominal}}{v_{nominal}} \times 100 $$ $$ e\,\% \;=\; \frac{25.641 - 25.000}{25.000} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{0.641}{25.000} \times 100 $$ $$ e\,\% \;\approx\; +2.56\,\% $$ **Interpretation:** The IEC 60601-2-25 standard and KTU laboratory protocol both mandate $e < \pm 2\%$. Since $+2.56\% > +2\%$, **the device FAILS calibration**. The student should adjust the reference frequency slightly downward (reduce $R_1$ marginally) until the next trial measures $< 2\%$ deviation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to write "$e = 2.56\%$" without the **sign**. Always include + or −. Positive means the paper is running *fast*; negative means *slow*. This affects the corrective action. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Off Table | Design Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | KTU Trade-Off | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gear ratio $G$ | Slower paper, higher torque | Faster paper, lower torque | Choose $G$ for torque margin | | PWM duty cycle $D$ | Higher avg. voltage, faster | Lower voltage, slower | Keep $D \in [40\%,\ 80\%]$ | | Encoder pulses/rev $p$ | Better speed resolution | Coarser resolution | $p \geq 100$ for ECG quality | | Supply voltage $V_s$ | Faster no-load speed | Slower speed | Match motor rating | | Reference frequency $f_{ref}$ | Faster chart speed | Slower chart speed | Tunable calibration knob | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Chart Drive ```mermaid graph LR A[REF_OSC

The reference oscillator defines the *target*. The motor + gearbox + roller produce the *actual* paper motion. The encoder feeds the *actual* motor speed back to the comparator, which closes the loop. The dotted mechanical-coupling line from the gear-box to the encoder is what makes this a true servo system. --- ### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic — 555 + Driver Topology ```mermaid graph TB VCC[VCC plus 12V DC] --> R1[R1 10 kohm to Pin 7] VCC --> R2A[R2A 10 kohm to Pin 7] R1 --> PIN7[Pin 7 Discharge] R2A --> R2B[R2B 10 kohm to Pin 6 and Pin 2] R2B --> PIN6[Pin 6 Threshold] R2B --> PIN2[Pin 2 Trigger] C1[Capacitor C 0.01 uF] --> GND1[GND] PIN6 --> C1 PIN8[Pin 8 VCC] --> VCC PIN4[Pin 4 Reset] --> VCC PIN1[Pin 1 GND] --> GND1 PIN3[Pin 3 Output] --> R3[Base Resistor R3 1 kohm] R3 --> Q1[Q1 TIP31C NPN Darlington Driver] Q1 --> MOT[Motor plus 12V Permanent Magnet DC] MOT --> FLY[Flyback Diode 1N4007 across Motor] MOT --> DIODE[D1 1N4007 Cathode to plus12V] SHAFT[Motor Shaft] --> GEAR[Gearbox G is 40 to 1] GEAR --> ROLLER[Chart Roller Diameter 12 mm] ROLLER --> PAPER[Thermal Chart Paper] SHAFT --> ENC[Optical Encoder 100 ppr] ENC --> FEEDBACK[Feedback Signal to Pin 5 Control Voltage] style VCC fill:#ffe1e1 style GND1 fill:#e1e1e1 style Q1 fill:#fff4e1 style MOT fill:#e1ffe1 style PAPER fill:#f0f0f0 ``` --- ### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram — Speed-Error Servo Logic ```mermaid graph TD S1[Start Command Power ON] --> S2[REF Enable 555 Astable Begins] S2 --> S3[Reference Frequency fref set] S3 --> S4[Compare fref vs ffb] S4 --> D1{Difference Zero} D1 -- Yes --> STABLE[Steady State Chart Speed Locked] D1 -- No Positive --> UP[Increase PWM Duty Cycle] D1 -- No Negative --> DN[Decrease PWM Duty Cycle] UP --> S5[Apply Corrected Voltage to Motor] DN --> S5 S5 --> S6[Motor Spins Faster or Slower] S6 --> S7[Encoder Reads New ffb] S7 --> S4 STABLE --> OUT[Output Uniform Chart Paper Trace] style S1 fill:#e1f5ff style STABLE fill:#e1ffe1 style OUT fill:#ffe1e1 ``` > **Interpretation for KTU Records:** This decision-loop diagram should be redrawn in the lab record under the heading *"Flow chart of paper-speed regulation."* It directly scores full marks in the "Working Principle" section of the experiment record. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a chart drive circuit. Why is a closed-loop feedback control essential in a clinical chart recorder rather than an open-loop constant-voltage drive?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A chart drive circuit is the electromechanical subsystem that moves the recording paper at a calibrated constant linear velocity in biomedical recorders such as ECG and EEG machines, providing a uniform time-axis for the recorded waveform. (1 mark) > > In an open-loop constant-voltage drive, the chart speed would vary with paper-load friction, stylus pressure, mains voltage fluctuations, and motor brush wear, directly distorting the time-interval information of the patient's signal — a clinical and medico-legal hazard. (1 mark) > > A closed-loop system uses a tachogenerator/encoder feedback to compare actual speed with a reference frequency; the resulting error continuously adjusts the motor's PWM duty cycle, holding the chart speed constant to within ±2% as mandated by IEC 60601-2-25. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the standard chart paper speeds used in (i) ECG, (ii) EEG, and (iii) Phonocardiograph recorders. What is the time equivalent of one small box (1 mm) and one large box (5 mm) on a standard ECG paper?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > (i) ECG: **25 mm/s**, (ii) EEG: **30 mm/s** (with selectable 15 and 60 mm/s), (iii) Phonocardiograph: **50 – 100 mm/s**. (1.5 marks) > > On a 25 mm/s ECG: one small box (1 mm) = 0.04 s, one large box (5 mm) = 0.20 s. (1.5 marks) --- ### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-level ESE Part-B questions carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between the two full questions. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop chart drive circuit used in an ECG machine. Identify each block and state the function of the tachogenerator/encoder in the loop.* > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam, refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):** 2 marks > > 1. **Reference Oscillator (555 astable or crystal):** Generates a precise reference frequency $f_{ref}$ that corresponds to the desired paper speed. (1 mark) > 2. **Phase Comparator / Error Detector:** Compares $f_{ref}$ with the feedback frequency $f_{fb}$ and produces an error voltage proportional to their difference. (1 mark) > 3. **PWM Generator / Motor Driver:** Converts the error into a variable-duty-cycle PWM signal that drives the DC motor. (1 mark) > 4. **DC Motor + Gearbox + Roller:** Actuator chain that converts electrical energy into uniform linear paper motion. (1 mark) > 5. **Tachogenerator / Optical Encoder Function:** Mounted on the motor shaft, it produces a sinusoidal or pulse train $f_{fb}$ whose frequency is *directly proportional to the instantaneous motor RPM*. This is fed back to the phase comparator, allowing the loop to "see" the actual speed and self-correct. (1 mark) > > **Working summary:** Any deviation between $f_{ref}$ and $f_{fb}$ (caused by paper load, drag, or voltage drift) is detected by the comparator, which increases or decreases the PWM duty cycle to the motor until $f_{fb} = f_{ref}$ — i.e., the paper speed is *locked* to the reference. (0.5 mark for working summary) --- **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** *A chart drive circuit uses a DC motor coupled to a 14 mm-diameter sprocket roller through a 50:1 gearbox. The motor shaft carries an optical encoder producing 200 pulses per revolution. The motor runs at 2400 rpm. Calculate: (i) feedback frequency, (ii) roller rpm, and (iii) linear chart speed in mm/s. Comment on whether the speed is suitable for ECG recording.* > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $D_r = 14$ mm, $G = 50$, $p = 200$ pulses/rev, $n_m = 2400$ rpm. > > **(i) Feedback frequency $f_{fb}$:** (2 marks) > > $$ > f_{fb} \;=\; \frac{n_m \times p}{60} \;=\; \frac{2400 \times 200}{60} \;=\; 8000~\text{Hz} > $$ > > **(ii) Roller rpm $n_r$:** (2 marks) > > $$ > n_r \;=\; \frac{n_m}{G} \;=\; \frac{2400}{50} \;=\; 48~\text{rpm} > $$ > > **(iii) Linear chart speed $v$:** (2 marks) > > $$ > v \;=\; \frac{\pi D_r \, n_r}{60} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 14 \times 48}{60} \;=\; \frac{2111.15}{60} \;\approx\; 35.19~\text{mm/s} > $$ > > **Comment (1 mark):** The calculated speed 35.19 mm/s is **faster** than the ECG standard of 25 mm/s. To achieve 25 mm/s, either the gear ratio must be increased to $G' = G \times (35.19/25) = 50 \times 1.408 \approx 70:1$, or the motor rpm must be reduced to $n_m' = 2400 \times (25/35.19) \approx 1705$ rpm. Since the standard allows only 25 mm/s for ECG, the **present setting is unsuitable for ECG** — it would compress the apparent heart rate by a factor of 0.71, leading to misdiagnosis. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Design a 555 timer-based astable multivibrator to generate the reference frequency for a chart drive circuit. The reference frequency required is 4 kHz and the timing capacitor chosen is $C = 0.01\ \mu F$. Calculate the timing resistors assuming a 50% duty cycle, and explain how this reference frequency is used to lock the motor speed.* > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **555 Astable Frequency Formula:** > > $$ > f_{ref} \;=\; \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C} > $$ > > Substituting $f_{ref} = 4000$ Hz and $C = 0.01\ \mu F = 10^{-8}$ F: (2 marks) > > $$ > R_1 + 2R_2 \;=\; \frac{1.44}{4000 \times 10^{-8}} \;=\; \frac{1.44}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 36000~\Omega > $$ > > For 50% duty cycle, $R_1 = R_2$: (2 marks) > > $$ > 3R_2 = 36000 \;\Rightarrow\; R_2 = 12~\text{k}\Omega > $$ > > Hence $R_1 = R_2 = 12~\text{k}\Omega$ (use standard 12 kΩ). > > **Locking mechanism (3 marks):** The 4 kHz reference frequency is fed to one input of a phase comparator. The motor-mounted encoder produces $f_{fb}$ at the second input. The comparator output, after low-pass filtering, controls the PWM duty cycle driving the motor. As long as $f_{fb} \neq f_{ref}$, an error voltage is generated; the motor's speed is varied until $f_{fb}$ exactly matches $f_{ref}$ at 4 kHz. At this point, the loop is *locked* and the chart speed is held constant. Any disturbance (load change, supply drift) momentarily unbalances the loop, and the feedback automatically re-establishes the lock. --- **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *During KTU lab calibration, a student measures that the chart paper advances 200 mm in 8.4 seconds when the recorder is set to 25 mm/s. (i) Calculate the percentage speed error. (ii) State whether the device passes KTU/IEC calibration. (iii) Suggest one practical corrective action to bring the error within tolerance.* > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **(i) Percentage error (3 marks):** > > $$ > v_{measured} \;=\; \frac{200}{8.4} \;\approx\; 23.81~\text{mm/s} > $$ > > $$ > e\,\% \;=\; \frac{23.81 - 25.00}{25.00} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{-1.19}{25} \times 100 \;\approx\; -4.76\,\% > $$ > > **(ii) Calibration verdict (2 marks):** The IEC 60601-2-25 standard and KTU lab protocol both require $|e| < 2\%$. Since $|-4.76\%| = 4.76\% > 2\%$, the device **FAILS calibration**. > > **(iii) Corrective action (2 marks):** The paper is running *slow* (negative error). The corrective action is to **slightly increase the reference frequency** $f_{ref}$ by a factor of $25/23.81 = 1.0499$, i.e., increase it by 5%. Practically, this is done by **reducing $R_1$ or $R_2$** in the 555 astable by ~5% (e.g., replace the 10 kΩ $R_1$ with a 9.5 kΩ trimmer potentiometer and readjust until the calibration check passes). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. **Forgetting units** on the final answer for chart speed (must be **mm/s**, not cm/s or m/s). Loss: 1 mark. > 2. **Sign of error** — writing "$e = 4.76\%$" without + or −. Loss: 0.5 mark. > 3. **Mixing up 555 formula** — writing $f = 1/(R_1 + 2R_2)C$ (which is for symmetric square wave, not 555). The **KTU-accepted form is $1.44/[(R_1 + 2R_2)C]$**. Loss: 1–2 marks. > 4. **Not commenting on clinical suitability** in design problems (e.g., "speed is 35 mm/s — not suitable for ECG"). Loss: 1 mark. > 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — examiners *expect* a diagram. Loss: 2 marks. --- ### 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam** - ☐ **Definition:** Chart drive = calibrated linear paper motion in a clinical recorder; *closed-loop*, not open-loop. - ☐ **ECG standard speed:** **25 mm/s**; EEG: **30 mm/s**; PCG: **50–100 mm/s**. - ☐ **Time conversions on ECG paper:** 1 mm = 0.04 s; 5 mm = 0.20 s. - ☐ **Core equations (4 must-know):** - $v = f_{fb} \cdot d_{pulse}$ - $n_r = n_m / G$ - $v = \pi D_r n_r / 60$ - $f = 1.44/[(R_1 + 2R_2) C]$ — **the 555 formula** - ☐ **Architecture (5 blocks):** Ref Osc → Comparator → PWM/Driver → Motor/Gear/Roller → Paper, with **encoder feedback to comparator**. - ☐ **Calibration tolerance:** $\pm 2\%$ (IEC 60601-2-25). - ☐ **Calibration procedure:** measure 100 mm with a stopwatch; compute $e\%$; correct by adjusting $f_{ref}$ (via $R_1, R_2$ of 555). - ☐ **PWM duty cycle** for chart drive: typically 50%–75% (2/3 is the design optimum). - ☐ **Pinch roller + sprocket** = non-slip drive mechanism. - ☐ **Always include sign (+/−) on percentage error.** - ☐ **Examiner pet topic:** "Why closed-loop and not open-loop?" — answer: load independence + clinical accuracy. - ☐ **Industrial relevance:** Philips, GE, Nihon Kohden, Mindray ECG machines use this architecture.

topics.title: QRS Detector Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML508 - CLINICAL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** QRS Detector Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QRS Complex (ECG):** The **QRS complex** is the single most dominant feature of the electrocardiogram (ECG) waveform, representing the rapid ventricular depolarization of the cardiac muscle. It is composed of three characteristic waves — the **Q wave** (initial downward deflection), the **R wave** (tall upward peak), and the **S wave** (downward deflection following R). It typically spans **80 ms to 120 ms** in duration for a healthy adult.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QRS Detector Circuit:** A **QRS Detector Circuit** is a dedicated analog/digital signal-processing front-end designed to isolate and extract the QRS complex from a noisy ECG trace by suppressing baseline wander, mains interference, and muscle artifacts. The output is a standardized **logic pulse** (typically a clean **5 V rectangular pulse of ~100 ms width**) synchronous with every heartbeat, used to trigger heart-rate counters, R-R interval timers, pacemakers, and defibrillators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a noisy stadium trying to listen to your friend's voice. Your brain automatically performs three tasks: **(i) filters out the background roar, (ii) focuses only on the loud "HELLO!" your friend shouts, and (iii) marks the exact moment you heard it**. 

A QRS detector does **exactly this** to the ECG waveform:

- The **stadium roar** is the **50 Hz power-line interference + baseline wander + EMG noise** superimposed on the ECG.
- Your **friend's "HELLO!"** is the **QRS complex**, which contains the highest frequency and largest slope in the entire cardiac cycle.
- The **pin on the calendar** marking the moment is the **clean rectangular output pulse** generated by the detector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why focus on the QRS only?**
> The QRS is the most electrically energetic event in the cardiac cycle. Its **steep slope (dV/dt)** and **dominant amplitude** make it mathematically easy to distinguish from P and T waves using **slope-and-amplitude detection**.

## 1.3 Standard Physiological & Electrical Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| QRS Duration (Adult) | **80 – 120 ms** |
| R-wave Amplitude (Lead II) | **1.0 – 1.5 mV** (typical) |
| Dominant QRS Frequency Band | **5 Hz – 15 Hz** |
| Peak Slope of R-wave | **Up to 1 mV/ms** |
| Normal Heart Rate | **60 – 100 bpm** |
| Mains Interference (India) | **50 Hz** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block-Level Architecture

A classical analog QRS detector is a cascade of **five cascaded stages**. Each stage performs a deterministic mathematical operation on the signal:

1. **Instrumentation Preamplifier** — Provides high CMRR (>80 dB) and differential gain (×1000) to lift the **1 mV ECG** to **1 V** level.
2. **Band-Pass Filter (BPF) — 5 Hz to 15 Hz** — Suppresses baseline drift (<5 Hz) and high-frequency EMG/mains noise (>15 Hz), isolating the QRS spectral band.
3. **Differentiator** — Computes the time-derivative (dV/dt) of the filtered signal. Because the QRS has the steepest slope, differentiation sharply amplifies it relative to P and T waves.
4. **Full-Wave Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)** — Rectifies the bipolar derivative into a unipolar signal so subsequent thresholding works on a single-polarity waveform.
5. **Schmitt Trigger / Threshold Comparator** — Generates a clean digital pulse whenever the rectified derivative exceeds a pre-set threshold.

## 2.2 Stage-Wise Mathematical Treatment

### Stage 1: Preamplifier Gain

$$
G_{\text{pre}} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{\text{gain}}}
$$

The differential output is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = G_{\text{pre}} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)
$$

### Stage 2: Band-Pass Filter

A **second-order active band-pass filter** is typically built by cascading a **2nd-order high-pass filter (HPF)** with a **2nd-order low-pass filter (LPF)**.

**High-Pass Section** (blocks baseline drift):

$$
f_{\text{L}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

**Low-Pass Section** (blocks high-frequency noise):

$$
f_{\text{H}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_3 R_4 C_3 C_4}}
$$

### Stage 3: Differentiator Transfer Function

The ideal differentiator has the transfer function:

$$
H(s) = -s R_f C_{\text{in}}
$$

For a sinusoidal input $V_{\text{in}}(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{\text{out}}(t) = -A \omega R_f C_{\text{in}} \cos(\omega t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The output amplitude is **proportional to frequency (ω)**. Since the QRS contains the highest frequencies, it gets amplified the most.

### Stage 4: Full-Wave Rectifier (Precision Absolute Value)

A precision rectifier using two op-amps eliminates the diode forward-voltage drop:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \vert V_{\text{in}} \vert
$$

### Stage 5: Schmitt Trigger Threshold

The threshold levels are:

$$
V_{\text{UT}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) + V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) \quad \text{(Upper Threshold)}
$$

$$
V_{\text{LT}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) - V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) \quad \text{(Lower Threshold)}
$$

Hysteresis width:

$$
V_H = V_{\text{UT}} - V_{\text{LT}} = 2 V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_i}
$$

### Heart-Rate Computation (System Output)

$$
\text{HR (bpm)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{RR}}$ is the time interval (in seconds) between two consecutive output pulses.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{\text{L}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Bandpass lower cutoff (HPF) | Hz |
| 2 | $f_{\text{H}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_3 R_4 C_3 C_4}}$ | Bandpass upper cutoff (LPF) | Hz |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{f_{\text{H}} - f_{\text{L}}}$ | Filter quality factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $H(s) = -sR_f C_{\text{in}}$ | Differentiator transfer function | — |
| 5 | $V_{\text{out}} = A\omega R_f C_{\text{in}}$ | Differentiator output amplitude | V |
| 6 | $V_{\text{UT}} = V_{\text{ref}}\!\left(1+\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) + V_{\text{sat}}\!\left(\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right)$ | Schmitt upper threshold | V |
| 7 | $V_{\text{LT}} = V_{\text{ref}}\!\left(1+\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) - V_{\text{sat}}\!\left(\frac{R_f}{R_i}\right)$ | Schmitt lower threshold | V |
| 8 | $V_H = 2V_{\text{sat}}\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Hysteresis width | V |
| 9 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}}$ | Heart rate in beats per minute | bpm |
| 10 | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** QRS detectors are the heart of every **bedside patient monitor, Holter recorder, AED (Automated External Defibrillator), implantable pacemaker, and ECG event recorder** in clinical practice today.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design of the 2nd-Order Butterworth Band-Pass Filter

The standard 2nd-order active LPF Sallen-Key topology has a transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_0}{Q} s + \omega_0^2}
$$

where $\omega_0$ is the natural (resonant) frequency and $Q$ is the quality factor. For a Butterworth response, **Q = 0.707**.

### Step 1 — Specify Design Constraints

We require:

- Lower cutoff $f_{\text{L}} = 5$ Hz
- Upper cutoff $f_{\text{H}} = 15$ Hz

### Step 2 — Compute the Center Frequency

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{f_{\text{L}} \cdot f_{\text{H}}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{5 \times 15} = \sqrt{75}
$$

$$
f_0 = 8.660\ \text{Hz}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Quality Factor

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{f_{\text{H}} - f_{\text{L}}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{8.660}{15 - 5} = \frac{8.660}{10}
$$

$$
Q = 0.866
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Angular Frequency

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 8.660
$$

$$
\omega_0 = 54.414\ \text{rad/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Choose Component Values

For equal-component design ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Choose $C = 100\ \text{nF}$:

$$
R = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{54.414 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{5.4414 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.8377 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
R \approx 184\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the conversion:** We used the equal-component approximation to reduce the biquadratic to a single equation, then solved for $R$ by fixing $C$ at a practical non-polarized value. The same procedure is applied independently to the LPF and HPF sections using their respective cutoff frequencies.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differentiator Time-Constant Selection

The non-ideal differentiator (with input resistor $R_1$ to prevent high-frequency noise amplification) has transfer function:

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_f C_1 s}{(1 + s R_1 C_1)(1 + s R_f C_2)}
$$

For the differentiation band (up to $f_{\text{H}} = 15$ Hz), the corner frequencies must lie **above** the QRS band. Choose $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$:

$$
f_{c1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_{c1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = 159.15\ \text{Hz}
$$

Since $f_{c1} = 159\ \text{Hz} \gg 15\ \text{Hz}$, the circuit behaves as a true differentiator inside the QRS band. Choose $R_f = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ for adequate gain.

Differentiator gain at QRS center frequency $f_0 = 8.66$ Hz:

$$
\vert H \vert = 2\pi f_0 R_f C_1 = 2\pi \times 8.66 \times 100{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\vert H \vert = 0.544
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Schmitt Trigger Threshold Setting

Design intent: trigger on the rising edge of the rectified derivative with **25 % overdrive** above noise.

Assume rectified peak derivative $V_{\text{rect,peak}} = 0.7$ V and noise floor $V_n = 0.1$ V.

Set threshold $V_{\text{th}} = V_n + 0.25 (V_{\text{rect,peak}} - V_n)$:

$$
V_{\text{th}} = 0.1 + 0.25(0.7 - 0.1) = 0.1 + 0.15
$$

$$
V_{\text{th}} = 0.25\ \text{V}
$$

For a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with $V_{\text{ref}} = 0$ V, $V_{\text{sat}} = \pm 13.5$ V (for $\pm 15$ V supply with op-amp):

$$
V_{\text{UT}} = +V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_i}
$$

For $V_{\text{UT}} = 0.25$ V:

$$
\frac{R_f}{R_i} = \frac{0.25}{13.5} = 0.01852
$$

Choose $R_i = 100\ \text{k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_f = 1.852\ \text{k}\Omega \approx 1.8\ \text{k}\Omega$ (standard value).

Hysteresis:

$$
V_H = 2 \times 13.5 \times 0.01852 = 0.500\ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hysteresis matters:** Without $V_H$, the comparator would re-trigger on residual ripple during the QRS complex, producing **multiple false pulses per heartbeat** — a classic student pitfall.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heart-Rate Computation Example

Suppose the output pulse train from the detector has a measured period of $T_{\text{RR}} = 0.857$ s (from oscilloscope):

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}} = \frac{60}{0.857}
$$

$$
\text{HR} = 70.01\ \text{bpm}
$$

This falls in the normal sinus rhythm range (**60–100 bpm**).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Stage on QRS

| Stage | Effect on R-wave | Effect on P-wave | Effect on T-wave | Effect on 50 Hz Noise |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preamplifier | Amplifies ×1000 | Amplifies ×1000 | Amplifies ×1000 | Rejected by CMRR |
| BPF (5–15 Hz) | Passes unchanged | Attenuates (P < 5 Hz strong) | Attenuates (T mostly < 5 Hz) | Rejects strongly |
| Differentiator | Amplifies (steep slope) | Slightly amplifies | Slightly amplifies | Further rejects |
| Rectifier | Unipolar pulse | Unipolar low pulse | Unipolar low pulse | Unipolar low ripple |
| Schmitt | Clean trigger | No trigger (below thresh.) | No trigger (below thresh.) | No trigger (hysteresis) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Functional Block Diagram of QRS Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ecgIn[ECG Electrodes] --> preamp[Stage1 Instrumentation Preamplifier]
    preamp --> bpf[Stage2 Band Pass Filter 5Hz to 15Hz]
    bpf --> diff[Stage3 Differentiator dV by dt]
    diff --> rect[Stage4 Full Wave Precision Rectifier]
    rect --> schmitt[Stage5 Schmitt Trigger Threshold Detector]
    schmitt --> mono[Monostable Multivibrator 100ms Pulse]
    mono --> hr[Heart Rate Display and RR Interval Counter]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Signal-Waveform Transformation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw["RAW ECG SIGNAL\nP QRS T waves with 50Hz noise\nBaseline drift present"]
    amp["AFTER PREAMP\nGain 1000\n1mV becomes 1V\nCMRR rejects mains"]
    filt["AFTER BANDPASS 5 to 15 Hz\nQRS dominant\nP and T attenuated"]
    deriv["AFTER DIFFERENTIATOR\nQRS produces large spike\nLow freq waves nearly zero"]
    rec["AFTER RECTIFIER\nUnipolar absolute value\nAll negative parts flipped"]
    trig["AFTER SCHMITT\nClean 5V rectangular pulse\n100 ms width"]
    out["OUTPUT PULSE TRAIN\nEach pulse equals one heartbeat\nFeeds HR counter"]

    raw --> amp --> filt --> deriv --> rec --> trig --> out
```

## 4.3 Complete Circuit Schematic — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[IN1 Lead I Positive] --> ia[IA AD620 Instrumentation Amp]
    nodeB[IN2 Lead I Negative] --> ia
    nodeC[REF Right Leg Drive] --> ia
    ia --> n1[Node G1 1000x Gain]
    n1 --> hpfc[HPF Sallen Key Fc 5Hz]
    hpfc --> n2[Node G2 AC Coupled]
    n2 --> lpfc[LPF Sallen Key Fc 15Hz]
    lpfc --> n3[Node G3 BPF Output]
    n3 --> diffOp[Op Amp Differentiator Cf 0.1uF Rf 100k]
    diffOp --> n4[Node G4 dV by dt]
    n4 --> pr[Precision Full Wave Rectifier Two Op Amps]
    pr --> n5[Node G5 Absolute Value]
    n5 --> st[Non Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold 0.25V]
    st --> n6[Node G6 5V Pulse]
    n6 --> mono[Monostable 555 Timer Width 100ms]
    mono --> n7[Node G7 Final Output]
    n7 --> disp[Digital Heart Rate Display bpm]
    n7 --> led[Visual LED Indicator Green on each beat]
```

## 4.4 Pin-Connected Op-Amp Internal Architecture (Schmitt Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inNode[Rectified Derivative Input] --> rIn[Ri 100k to Non Inverting]
    rIn --> nInv[Non Inverting Pin of Op Amp]
    nInv --> opaCore[Op Amp LM741]
    outNode[Inverting Input] --> rfb[Rf 1.8k Feedback]
    rfb --> nInv
    opaCore --> vout[5V Pulse Output]
    vout --> rfb
    vout --> nOut[To Monostable and HR Counter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** This block-level schematic represents the most common analog QRS detector architecture used in the KTU PCBML508 laboratory experiment. Modern implementations replace the analog chain with digital DSP running the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm**, but the underlying mathematical principles — bandpass filtering, differentiation, squaring, moving-window integration — remain identical.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the QRS complex. Why is the 5–15 Hz bandpass region chosen for QRS detection?**

**Model Answer:**
The QRS complex is the waveform representing ventricular depolarization in the ECG, with a typical duration of **80–120 ms** and a dominant frequency content of **5–15 Hz**. This band is selected because:
- Frequencies **below 5 Hz** correspond to baseline wander and the slow P and T waves.
- Frequencies **above 15 Hz** are dominated by EMG artifact and 50 Hz mains interference.
- The QRS contains the **steepest slope** in the ECG, whose spectral energy is concentrated precisely in the 5–15 Hz window, making it the optimal detection band.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks band justification)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the role of the differentiator stage in a QRS detector circuit?**

**Model Answer:**
The differentiator computes the time-derivative (dV/dt) of the bandpass-filtered ECG. Its role is to **amplify the QRS complex relative to P and T waves** because the QRS has the steepest slope in the cardiac cycle. Since the output of a differentiator is proportional to the input frequency ($V_{\text{out}} = A\omega R_f C$), higher-frequency components of the QRS produce a much larger output than the slower P and T waves. This greatly improves the signal-to-noise ratio before thresholding.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a **QRS detector circuit**. Discuss the role of each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **2nd-order active band-pass filter** with $f_{\text{L}} = 5$ Hz and $f_{\text{H}} = 15$ Hz using the equal-component Sallen-Key topology. Calculate the required resistor value assuming $C = 100$ nF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A QRS detector circuit extracts the QRS complex from a raw ECG signal and converts it into a clean rectangular pulse synchronous with every heartbeat. The block diagram consists of the following cascaded stages:

1. **Instrumentation Preamplifier (AD620 / INA128):** Provides a differential gain of ~1000 and a CMRR > 80 dB. It converts the 1 mV ECG to a 1 V signal and rejects common-mode 50 Hz noise.

2. **Band-Pass Filter (5 Hz – 15 Hz):** Implemented as cascaded Sallen-Key high-pass and low-pass sections. The HPF eliminates baseline wander and DC offset; the LPF suppresses EMG and high-frequency noise. Together, they pass only the QRS-dominant band.

3. **Differentiator:** Implemented using an op-amp with capacitive input and resistive feedback. Its transfer function $H(s) = -sR_fC$ amplifies the QRS because of its steep slope (high dV/dt).

4. **Full-Wave Precision Rectifier:** Built with two op-amps and two diodes to eliminate the diode forward-voltage drop. It converts the bipolar derivative into a unipolar absolute value, which is required for single-threshold detection.

5. **Schmitt Trigger / Threshold Comparator:** A non-inverting comparator with positive feedback. The hysteresis ($V_H$) prevents multiple triggering from residual ripple. It produces a clean 5 V pulse on every QRS detection.

6. **Monostable Multivibrator (optional):** A 555-timer-based monostable generates a standardized **100 ms rectangular pulse** regardless of input pulse width variation, ensuring uniform counting.

The output pulse train is finally fed to a heart-rate counter that computes $\text{HR} = 60 / T_{\text{RR}}$ in bpm. *(7 marks — 1 mark diagram + 1 mark per stage × 5 + 1 mark for output computation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f_{\text{L}} = 5$ Hz, $f_{\text{H}} = 15$ Hz, $C = 100$ nF

**Step 1 — Center frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{f_{\text{L}} \cdot f_{\text{H}}} = \sqrt{5 \times 15} = \sqrt{75} = 8.660\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Quality factor:**

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{f_{\text{H}} - f_{\text{L}}} = \frac{8.660}{15 - 5} = 0.866
$$

**Step 3 — Angular frequency:**

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 8.660 = 54.414\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Resistor value (equal-component design):**

$$
R = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{54.414 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.838 \times 10^5\ \Omega \approx 184\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Standard resistor value:**

The nearest E12 standard value is **180 kΩ**, giving a small design tolerance of ~2 %. *(7 marks — 1 mark formula + 1 mark each for f₀, Q, ω₀, R + 1 mark for standard value)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Schmitt trigger circuit** as used in a QRS detector. Derive the equations for upper and lower threshold voltages and explain the importance of hysteresis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The output pulse train of a QRS detector has a period of $T_{\text{RR}} = 0.75$ s. Compute the heart rate in bpm. If a patient shows a heart rate of **120 bpm**, what is the corresponding $T_{\text{RR}}$ in seconds and in milliseconds? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with positive feedback that converts a slowly varying input into a clean binary output. In a QRS detector, it acts as the **threshold-detection stage** that fires only when the rectified derivative exceeds a set voltage.

**Circuit Topology:** Non-inverting configuration with input applied to the non-inverting terminal and feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to the non-inverting terminal. A resistor $R_i$ connects the non-inverting terminal to a reference $V_{\text{ref}}$. The inverting terminal is grounded.

**KCL at the non-inverting terminal:**

$$
\frac{V_{\text{ref}} - V^+}{R_i} + \frac{V_{\text{out}} - V^+}{R_f} = 0
$$

Solving for $V^+$:

$$
V^+ = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_i + R_f} + V_{\text{out}} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}
$$

When $V_{\text{out}} = +V_{\text{sat}}$:

$$
V_{\text{UT}} = V^+ = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_i + R_f} + V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}
$$

When $V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{sat}}$:

$$
V_{\text{LT}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_i + R_f} - V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}
$$

Hysteresis width:

$$
V_H = V_{\text{UT}} - V_{\text{LT}} = 2 V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}
$$

**Importance of Hysteresis:**
- Prevents **multiple false triggers** caused by ripple and noise riding on the rectified QRS pulse.
- Ensures **one clean pulse per heartbeat**, essential for accurate heart-rate counting.
- Provides **noise immunity** of magnitude $V_H$ — small fluctuations below $V_H$ are ignored. *(7 marks — 2 marks circuit + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks hysteresis importance)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Case 1 — Given $T_{\text{RR}} = 0.75$ s, find HR:**

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}} = \frac{60}{0.75}
$$

$$
\text{HR} = 80\ \text{bpm}
$$

This lies in the **normal sinus rhythm** range (60–100 bpm).

**Case 2 — Given HR = 120 bpm, find $T_{\text{RR}}$:**

$$
T_{\text{RR}} = \frac{60}{\text{HR}} = \frac{60}{120}
$$

$$
T_{\text{RR}} = 0.5\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to milliseconds:

$$
T_{\text{RR}} = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500\ \text{ms}
$$

**Clinical Note:** A heart rate of 120 bpm corresponds to **tachycardia** (HR > 100 bpm). The corresponding R-R interval of 500 ms can be measured directly on the oscilloscope as the time between two successive output pulses. *(7 marks — 2 marks Case 1 + 2 marks Case 2 + 1 mark conversion + 2 marks clinical interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in QRS Detector Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Preamplifier and Instrumentation Amplifier:** Students often write "amplifier" without specifying **CMRR** — a mandatory specification for any clinical ECG front-end. Always mention CMRR > 80 dB.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert Hz to rad/s** (or vice-versa) in filter design. The factor of $2\pi$ is the single most common source of numerical errors in the exam.
> 3. **Omitting hysteresis in the Schmitt trigger** — examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for explicitly explaining why $V_H$ is needed.
> 4. **Mixing up $f_{\text{L}}$ and $f_{\text{H}}$:** Remember, the **HPF sets the lower cutoff** (5 Hz) and the **LPF sets the upper cutoff** (15 Hz). Swapping them will invert the entire filter response.
> 5. **Not mentioning monostable pulse standardization** — for HR counters, a fixed 100 ms pulse width is required; if you forget this, you'll lose the "output conditioning" mark.
> 6. **Units mistake in HR formula:** HR is in **bpm**, so $T_{\text{RR}}$ **must be in seconds** in the denominator — students often plug in milliseconds and get HR = 60000 bpm!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — QRS Detector Circuit (PCBML508 Module 2)**

- **QRS Duration:** **80–120 ms**; represents ventricular depolarization.
- **QRS Spectral Band:** Dominant energy lies between **5 Hz and 15 Hz** — this defines the BPF pass-band.
- **Five Cascaded Stages:** Preamplifier → Band-Pass Filter → Differentiator → Precision Rectifier → Schmitt Trigger.
- **Preamplifier:** Instrumentation amp (AD620), gain ≈ 1000, CMRR > 80 dB, input impedance > 10 MΩ.
- **Band-Pass Filter:** Cascaded 2nd-order Sallen-Key HPF (5 Hz) + LPF (15 Hz). Center frequency $f_0 = \sqrt{f_{\text{L}} f_{\text{H}}} = 8.66$ Hz.
- **Differentiator:** Transfer function $H(s) = -sR_fC_{\text{in}}$; output proportional to **frequency × amplitude** — exploits QRS's high slope.
- **Precision Rectifier:** Two op-amp topology eliminates diode drop; output is the **absolute value** of the input.
- **Schmitt Trigger:** Non-inverting with positive feedback; thresholds $V_{\text{UT}}$ and $V_{\text{LT}}$; hysteresis $V_H = 2V_{\text{sat}}R_f/R_i$.
- **Hysteresis is mandatory** to prevent multiple triggering from ripple.
- **Monostable 555 Timer:** Standardizes output pulse width to **100 ms**.
- **Heart Rate Formula:** $\text{HR (bpm)} = 60 / T_{\text{RR}} \text{ (seconds)}$.
- **Normal HR:** 60–100 bpm; **Bradycardia** < 60 bpm; **Tachycardia** > 100 bpm.
- **Real-world Applications:** Bedside monitors, Holter recorders, AEDs, implantable pacemakers, cardiac event recorders.
- **Digital Equivalent:** The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** uses the same mathematical principles (BPF → derivative → squaring → moving-window integration) implemented in software on DSP/FPGA platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Gain Compensation Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML508 - CLINICAL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Gain Compensation Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Gain Compensation (AGC) Circuit:** A closed-loop, negative-feedback electronic subsystem that continuously monitors the amplitude of an output signal and automatically adjusts the gain of a preceding variable-gain amplifier so that the output signal level is maintained at a near-constant, predefined reference level, irrespective of variations in the input signal amplitude.

In clinical instrumentation, the AGC is also referred to as **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** or **Automatic Level Control (ALC)**. It is a critical building block in bio-signal acquisition chains (ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG) where physiological signal amplitudes can vary by an order of magnitude between patients (e.g., ECG R-wave amplitude ranges from **0.5 mV to 4 mV**) or even within the same patient over time due to electrode impedance changes, motion artefacts, and respiration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Auto-Brightness Smartphone Camera":**
> Imagine you are taking photographs outdoors. When the sun is extremely bright, the camera automatically darkens its sensor sensitivity. When you move into a dim room, it automatically brightens the sensor. You (the user) always receive a properly exposed photograph regardless of ambient light. The **Automatic Gain Compensation Circuit does the exact same thing for electrical signals** — when the bio-signal becomes too large, the circuit "dims" the amplifier; when the signal becomes too small, it "brightens" the amplifier, so the recorder/display always sees a uniform, usable signal level.

### 1.3 Why AGC is Indispensable in Clinical Instrumentation

- **Patient-to-patient variability:** A 70 kg adult and a 5 kg neonate have radically different cardiac potentials.
- **Electrode-skin interface drift:** Impedance changes with sweat, motion, and time.
- **Dynamic range compression:** The A/D converter (ADC) of the monitoring system has a fixed input range (e.g., **±5 V**). AGC keeps the bio-signal within this window.
- **Display readability:** Ensures the trace on the CRT / LCD monitor / chart recorder remains consistently sized.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics of an AGC Circuit

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Clinical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference Output Level | $V_{REF}$ | **1 V peak** |
| Control Range (Dynamic Range) | $DR$ | **40 dB to 60 dB** |
| Settling Time | $t_{s}$ | **0.5 s to 2 s** |
| Time Constant of Envelope Detector | $\tau$ | **0.1 s to 1 s** |
| Compression Ratio | $CR$ | **> 20:1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The Module 2 lab experiment typically uses an op-amp based AGC realized with a **JFET / FET used as a voltage-controlled resistor (VCR)** in the feedback path of an inverting amplifier, combined with a **precision rectifier (envelope detector)** and a **DC comparator / error amplifier** to close the feedback loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Five Functional Blocks

The AGC loop is composed of five distinct stages, each fulfilling a precise role:

- **(a) Variable Gain Amplifier (VGA) — the "Plant"**
  - A differential or inverting amplifier whose gain $A_v$ is *electronically tunable* by an external DC control voltage $V_C$.
  - In the standard KTU lab implementation, the gain is varied by changing the **drain-to-source resistance $r_{DS}$ of a JFET** acting as the input (or feedback) resistor. The transfer function is:
  $$A_v(V_C) \;=\; -\frac{R_F}{R_{in}} \quad \text{(fixed resistor case)}$$
  $$A_v(V_C) \;=\; -\frac{R_F}{r_{DS}(V_{GS})} \quad \text{(JFET-VCR case)}$$

- **(b) Precision Rectifier / Peak Detector — the "Sensor"**
  - Converts the AC output of the VGA into a proportional DC envelope voltage.
  - Built using an op-amp, two signal diodes (e.g., **1N4148**), and an RC holding network.
  - The output $V_{pk}$ tracks the **peak amplitude** of $V_{out}(t)$:
  $$V_{pk}(t) \;\approx\; \vert V_{out}(t) \vert_{peak}$$

- **(c) Error Amplifier / Comparator — the "Controller"**
  - A summing difference amplifier that compares the detected envelope $V_{pk}$ against a fixed DC reference $V_{REF}$.
  - Produces an error voltage:
  $$V_{\varepsilon} \;=\; K_{\varepsilon}\,\bigl(V_{REF} - V_{pk}\bigr)$$
  - Here, $K_{\varepsilon}$ is the error amplifier's gain (typically **1 to 10**).

- **(d) DC Control / Integrator Stage — the "Actuator / Compensator"**
  - Often realized as an **integrator** (op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_i$) to ensure smooth, drift-free control and to set the loop time-constant.
  - Output is the control voltage $V_C$ applied to the gate of the JFET:
  $$V_C(t) \;=\; -\frac{1}{R_i C_i}\int V_{\varepsilon}(t)\,dt$$
  - For a P-controller (proportional, no integrator):
  $$V_C \;=\; K_p \cdot V_{\varepsilon}$$

- **(e) Feedback Path — the "Loop Closure"**
  - The control voltage $V_C$ is routed back to the gate of the JFET in the VGA, closing the negative-feedback loop. Any increase in $V_{out}$ produces a control action that **reduces** $A_v$, and vice-versa.

### 2.2 Closed-Loop Mathematical Behaviour — The Core "Why"

In the steady-state (loop-settled) condition, the negative feedback enforces a balance:

$$V_{pk} \;\rightarrow\; V_{REF} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;\approx\; V_{REF} \;=\; \text{constant}$$

This is the **fundamental law of any AGC**: the output peak is forced to a constant reference. The input amplitude $V_{in}$ can swing over a wide range, but $V_{out}$ is *clamped* at $V_{REF}$.

### 2.3 Steady-State Gain as a Function of Input

Since $A_v = V_{REF} / V_{in,peak}$ at equilibrium, the effective closed-loop gain becomes **input-dependent** and exhibits a logarithmic compression characteristic:

$$A_{v,closed}(V_{in}) \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{V_{in,peak}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{V_{in,peak}}$$

In decibels:

$$A_{v,closed}^{dB} \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{REF}}{V_{in,peak}}\right)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every equation you must memorize for the KTU University Exam and the lab viva voce.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open-loop gain (JFET-VCR) | $A_v = -R_F / r_{DS}$ | dimensionless | VGA transfer gain |
| 2 | JFET VCR relation | $r_{DS} = r_{DS,0}\,(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^{-1}$ | ohms ($\Omega$) | Drain-source resistance vs gate bias |
| 3 | Peak detector output | $V_{pk} = \vert V_{out} \vert_{peak}$ | volts (V) | Detected envelope |
| 4 | Error voltage | $V_{\varepsilon} = K_{\varepsilon}\,(V_{REF} - V_{pk})$ | volts (V) | Loop error signal |
| 5 | Integrator control | $V_C(t) = -(1/R_i C_i)\int V_{\varepsilon}\,dt$ | volts (V) | Smoothed control voltage |
| 6 | Steady-state output | $\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} = V_{REF}$ | volts (V) | The defining AGC equation |
| 7 | Closed-loop gain | $A_{v,cl} = V_{REF}/V_{in,peak}$ | dimensionless | Effective gain |
| 8 | Compression ratio | $CR = \Delta V_{in} / \Delta V_{out}$ | dimensionless | Input swing / output swing |
| 9 | Time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) | Envelope detector bandwidth |
| 10 | Settling time (5%) | $t_s \approx 3\tau$ | seconds (s) | Loop response speed |
| 11 | Dynamic range | $DR = 20\log_{10}(V_{in,max}/V_{in,min})$ | decibels (dB) | AGC working range |
| 12 | Loop gain (open) | $T = K_{\varepsilon}\cdot A_v \cdot S$ | dimensionless | Stability criterion |

Where $S$ is the sensitivity of $r_{DS}$ to $V_{GS}$ (in $\Omega/\text{V}$), and $V_P$ is the JFET pinch-off voltage (a negative quantity, typically **−2 V to −8 V**).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AGC is the *silent workhorse* of every modern clinical monitor. Beyond ECG/EEG/EMG, the same topology appears in:
> - **Pulse oximeters (PPG):** Compensates for perfusion index variations.
> - **Hearing aids:** Compresses the 120 dB audible range into the patient's usable dynamic range.
> - **FM radio receivers:** Standard IF-stage AGC at **10.7 MHz**.
> - **Ultrasound scanners:** Time-gain compensation (TGC) is a 1-D generalization of AGC along the time/depth axis.
> - **Defibrillators:** Preamplifier AGC prevents saturation during the high-voltage discharge artefact.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Output Equation

**Goal:** Show that in closed-loop equilibrium, $\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} = V_{REF}$.

**Step 1 — Define the input signal.** Let the bio-signal entering the VGA be a sinusoid of variable amplitude (representing the worst-case varying R-wave):

$$V_{in}(t) \;=\; V_{in,peak}\,\sin(\omega t) \qquad [\text{volts}]$$

where $V_{in,peak}$ is a slowly varying parameter (the *envelope* of the bio-signal).

**Step 2 — Write the VGA output.** The VGA multiplies the input by its tunable gain $A_v(V_C)$:

$$V_{out}(t) \;=\; A_v(V_C) \cdot V_{in}(t) \;=\; A_v(V_C) \cdot V_{in,peak} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

The peak of this output is therefore:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;=\; \vert A_v(V_C) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the peak detector.** Assuming an ideal precision rectifier with unity gain, the peak detector outputs a DC level equal to the peak of $V_{out}$:

$$V_{pk} \;=\; \vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;=\; \vert A_v(V_C) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the error amplifier.** The error amplifier generates:

$$V_{\varepsilon} \;=\; K_{\varepsilon}\,\bigl(V_{REF} - V_{pk}\bigr) \;=\; K_{\varepsilon}\,\Bigl(V_{REF} - \vert A_v(V_C) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak}\Bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the integrator (control law).** Differentiating both sides of the integrator equation (since the integrator output is $V_C$):

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{1}{R_i C_i}\,V_{\varepsilon} \;=\; -\frac{K_{\varepsilon}}{R_i C_i}\,\Bigl(V_{REF} - \vert A_v(V_C) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak}\Bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Invoke the steady-state condition.** At equilibrium, the control voltage has stopped changing, so $dV_C/dt = 0$:

$$0 \;=\; -\frac{K_{\varepsilon}}{R_i C_i}\,\Bigl(V_{REF} - \vert A_v(V_C^{\ast}) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak}\Bigr)$$

Since $K_{\varepsilon}$, $R_i$, and $C_i$ are all non-zero finite constants, the term in parentheses must vanish:

$$V_{REF} - \vert A_v(V_C^{\ast}) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the output peak:**

$$\boxed{\;\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;=\; \vert A_v(V_C^{\ast}) \vert \cdot V_{in,peak} \;=\; V_{REF}\;}$$

This is the **defining result** of any AGC: the output peak is locked to the reference. The corresponding equilibrium control voltage $V_C^{\ast}$ adjusts itself so that the JFET's $r_{DS}$ produces exactly the gain $A_v$ needed to make this equation true, *regardless* of $V_{in,peak}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain and Compression Ratio

**Step 1 — Solve the steady-state equation for $A_v$:**

$$\vert A_v(V_C^{\ast}) \vert \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{V_{in,peak}}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that this is the effective closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{v,cl} \;=\; \frac{V_{out,peak}}{V_{in,peak}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{V_{in,peak}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the compression ratio.** Suppose the input swings from $V_{in,1}$ to $V_{in,2}$ (with $V_{in,2} > V_{in,1}$). The output remains at $V_{REF}$ in both cases, so:

$$CR \;=\; \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{\Delta V_{out}} \;=\; \frac{V_{in,2} - V_{in,1}}{V_{REF} - V_{REF}} \;\rightarrow\; \infty$$

In practice, $V_{out}$ varies by a small amount $\delta$ (loop-tracking error), so:

$$CR \;\approx\; \frac{V_{in,2} - V_{in,1}}{2\,\delta} \quad \text{(typically 20:1 to 50:1)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Loop Time-Constant and Settling Time

The dominant time-constant of the AGC loop is the time-constant of the envelope detector (assuming it is slower than the integrator and the VGA):

$$\tau_{det} \;=\; R_{det}\cdot C_{det}$$

The integrator contributes an additional pole:

$$\tau_{int} \;=\; R_i \cdot C_i$$

The **closed-loop settling time to within 5%** of the final value is, by standard first-order system theory:

$$t_s \;\approx\; 3\,(\tau_{det} + \tau_{int})$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variation of Gain with Input Amplitude

The following table traces the loop behaviour as the input amplitude is stepped from 50 mV to 500 mV (a 20 dB step), assuming $V_{REF} = 1\text{ V}$, $K_{\varepsilon} = 1$, and integrator time-constant $\tau = 0.5$ s.

| $V_{in,peak}$ (mV) | Initial $V_{out,peak}$ (V) | $V_{\varepsilon}$ (V) | $V_C$ adjustment | Final $A_v$ | Final $V_{out,peak}$ (V) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 50 | 0.05 | $+0.95$ | $V_C$ decreases | $r_{DS}$ increases, $A_v$ rises | $1.00$ |
| 100 | 0.10 | $+0.90$ | $V_C$ decreases slowly | $A_v = 10$ | $1.00$ |
| 200 | 0.20 | $+0.80$ | $V_C$ decreases less | $A_v = 5$ | $1.00$ |
| 500 | 0.50 | $+0.50$ | $V_C$ stabilizes | $A_v = 2$ | $1.00$ |
| 1000 | 1.00 | $0$ | $V_C$ steady | $A_v = 1$ | $1.00$ |

> **Key observation:** The output is pinned at **1.00 V** in all rows. The gain $A_v$ automatically *inverts* its value to compensate for the input — this is the essence of automatic gain compensation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of the AGC Loop

The diagram below depicts the five-stage closed-loop AGC architecture with the negative-feedback path explicitly highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Variable Bio Signal] --> B[VGA Variable Gain Amplifier JFET VCR]
    B --> C[Vout Regulated Output Signal]
    C --> D[Precision Rectifier and Peak Detector]
    D --> E[DC Envelope Signal Vpk]
    E --> F[Error Amplifier and Comparator]
    G[DC Reference Voltage Vref] --> F
    F --> H[Error Signal Vepsilon]
    H --> I[Integrator and Control Stage]
    I --> J[Control Voltage Vc]
    J -.Negative Feedback.-> B

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style E fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style F fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca
    style G fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style H fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca
    style I fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9
    style J fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9
```

### 4.2 Circuit-Level Schematic (Op-Amp Realization with JFET-VCR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VGA[Variable Gain Amplifier Stage]
        VinNode[Vin] --> RinNode[Rin Fixed Resistor]
        RinNode --> InvNode[Inverting Node of OpAmp A1]
        JFETNode[JFET 2N3819 VCR rDS] --> InvNode
        InvNode --> AmpOut[A1 Output Vout]
        AmpOut --> FBGate[Gate of JFET receives Vc]
    end

    subgraph PD[Peak Detector Stage]
        AmpOut --> PDIn[Precision Rectifier A2 plus Diodes]
        PDIn --> HoldCap[Hold Capacitor Ch and Discharge Rh]
        HoldCap --> VpkNode[Vpk DC Envelope]
    end

    subgraph ERR[Error Amplifier Stage]
        VpkNode --> SummingNode[Summing Junction]
        VrefNode[Vref from Zener or Potentiometer] --> SummingNode
        SummingNode --> A3Node[Error Amplifier A3 Gain Kepsilon]
        A3Node --> VepsilonNode[Vepsilon]
    end

    subgraph INT[Integrator Control Stage]
        VepsilonNode --> RintNode[Resistor Rint]
        RintNode --> A4InNode[Inverting Input of Integrator A4]
        CintNode[Feedback Capacitor Cint] --> A4InNode
        A4InNode --> VcOutNode[Vc Control Voltage]
    end

    VcOutNode -.feedback wire.-> FBGate
    VpkNode -.feedback wire.-> SummingNode

    style VGA fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style PD fill:#fdf2f8,stroke:#9d174d
    style ERR fill:#eef2ff,stroke:#4338ca
    style INT fill:#f5f3ff,stroke:#6d28d9
    style FBGate fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207
```

### 4.3 Signal-Waveform Timeline (How the Loop Reacts to an Input Step)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T0 Input jumps 50 mV to 500 mV] --> T1[T0 to T1 plus 0 to 0.2s VGA output momentarily saturates near 5V]
    T1 --> T2[T1 to T2 plus 0.2 to 1.5s Peak detector rises and integrator adjusts Vc downward]
    T2 --> T3[T2 to T3 plus 1.5 to 2.5s rDS rises and A v drops to 2 and output falls toward Vref]
    T3 --> T4[T2 plus 2.5s onward Steady state Vout equals Vref equals 1V constant]

    style T0 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style T1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style T2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c
    style T3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d
    style T4 fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** The KTU 2024 lab manual uses a hardware realization in which a JFET (2N3819 or BF245) functions as a Voltage-Controlled Resistor (VCR). The op-amp ICs typically employed are the **µA741** (general purpose) or **LF353 / TL082** (JFET-input, lower bias current). The peak detector uses a **1N4148** signal diode pair, and the reference voltage is derived from a **zener diode (e.g., 5.1 V)** or a potentiometer across a stable supply.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Automatic Gain Compensation (AGC). State **two** reasons why AGC is essential in a clinical ECG amplifier.

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Automatic Gain Compensation (AGC)** is a closed-loop negative-feedback circuit that automatically adjusts the gain of an amplifier so that its output amplitude is maintained at a near-constant, predefined reference level, irrespective of variations in the input signal amplitude.
>
> **Two reasons for AGC in an ECG amplifier:**
> 1. **Patient-to-patient variability:** The R-wave amplitude varies from **0.5 mV to 4 mV** between a neonate and an adult; AGC normalizes this for uniform display.
> 2. **Electrode impedance drift:** Skin-electrode impedance varies with sweat, motion, and time, causing baseline wander and amplitude fluctuation; AGC compensates dynamically.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the **four** functional blocks of a typical AGC circuit. Briefly state the role of each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Block | Role |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Variable Gain Amplifier (VGA)** | Amplifies the input by a gain that is electronically tunable by a DC control voltage. |
| 2 | **Peak Detector / Envelope Detector** | Rectifies and smooths the AC output to produce a DC envelope voltage proportional to the output peak. |
| 3 | **Error Amplifier / Comparator** | Compares the detected envelope against a fixed reference and produces an error signal. |
| 4 | **Integrator / Control Stage** | Integrates the error signal to produce the smooth control voltage that drives the VGA. |

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Mandate (KTU 2024 ESE):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Automatic Gain Compensation circuit** used in a clinical instrumentation system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression showing that the steady-state peak output voltage of the AGC loop is equal to the reference voltage $V_{REF}$, assuming an ideal integrator in the control path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**

The AGC circuit consists of four functional blocks connected in a closed negative-feedback loop.

- **Variable Gain Amplifier (VGA):** A JFET (2N3819) acts as a voltage-controlled resistor $r_{DS}$ in the feedback path of op-amp A1. The voltage gain is $A_v = -R_F / r_{DS}$. As the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ (which equals the control voltage $V_C$ referenced to ground) becomes more negative, $r_{DS}$ increases, and the gain drops.
- **Peak Detector:** A precision half-wave rectifier (op-amp A2 with diodes D1, D2) followed by a hold capacitor $C_h$ and discharge resistor $R_h$ produces a DC envelope $V_{pk} = \vert V_{out} \vert_{peak}$.
- **Error Amplifier:** Op-amp A3 sums $V_{pk}$ (inverting input) with a fixed DC reference $V_{REF}$ (non-inverting input), producing an error voltage $V_{\varepsilon} = K_{\varepsilon}(V_{REF} - V_{pk})$.
- **Integrator:** Op-amp A4 with $R_i$ and $C_i$ integrates the error voltage to produce the smooth control voltage $V_C = -(1/R_i C_i)\int V_{\varepsilon}\,dt$, which is fed back to the gate of the JFET.

The reference block diagram is:

```
Vin → [VGA] → Vout → [Peak Detector] → Vpk → (+) [Error Amp] (–) ← Vref
                       ↑                                       ↓
                       └──────────── [Integrator] ← Vε ←───────┘
                                                  ↓
                                                  Vc → JFET gate (closes loop)
```

**Working Principle:** If $V_{in,peak}$ suddenly increases, $V_{out,peak}$ momentarily rises above $V_{REF}$, making $V_{\varepsilon}$ negative. The integrator ramps $V_C$ *more negative*, increasing $r_{DS}$ and *reducing* the VGA gain until $V_{out,peak}$ is pulled back down to $V_{REF}$. The opposite action occurs for a sudden decrease in $V_{in,peak}$. Hence the output is automatically maintained at the reference level.

**(b) Mathematical Derivation (7 marks):**

The output of the VGA is:

$$V_{out}(t) \;=\; -\frac{R_F}{r_{DS}(V_{GS})}\,V_{in}(t)$$

The peak detector gives:

$$V_{pk} \;=\; \frac{R_F}{r_{DS}(V_C)}\,V_{in,peak}$$

The error amplifier output:

$$V_{\varepsilon} \;=\; K_{\varepsilon}\,\bigl(V_{REF} - V_{pk}\bigr) \;=\; K_{\varepsilon}\!\left(V_{REF} - \frac{R_F \, V_{in,peak}}{r_{DS}(V_C)}\right)$$

The integrator equation:

$$V_C(t) \;=\; -\frac{1}{R_i C_i}\int_{0}^{t} V_{\varepsilon}(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; V_C(0)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{V_{\varepsilon}}{R_i C_i} \;=\; -\frac{K_{\varepsilon}}{R_i C_i}\!\left(V_{REF} - \frac{R_F \, V_{in,peak}}{r_{DS}(V_C)}\right)$$

At steady state, the control voltage settles, so:

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} \;=\; 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; V_{REF} - \frac{R_F \, V_{in,peak}}{r_{DS}(V_C^{\ast})} \;=\; 0$$

Solving for the output peak:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;=\; \frac{R_F \, V_{in,peak}}{r_{DS}(V_C^{\ast})} \;=\; V_{REF}$$

$$\boxed{\;\vert V_{out} \vert_{peak} \;=\; V_{REF}\;}$$

This confirms the defining property of an AGC: the output peak equals the reference, *independent* of the input amplitude $V_{in,peak}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain how a **JFET can be used as a Voltage-Controlled Resistor (VCR)** in the feedback path of an op-amp to realize a variable-gain stage. Mention the role of pinch-off voltage $V_P$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinical AGC circuit uses a VGA with $R_F = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and a JFET whose $r_{DS}$ varies from **200 Ω to 20 kΩ** as the control voltage $V_C$ varies from **0 V to −4 V**. The reference voltage is $V_{REF} = 1\text{ V}$. Determine (i) the maximum and minimum closed-loop gains, and (ii) the range of input signal amplitudes over which the AGC can maintain $V_{out,peak} = 1\text{ V}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) JFET as a VCR (7 marks):**

A JFET operated in the **ohmic (triode) region** — i.e., with $\vert V_{DS}\vert < \vert V_{GS} - V_P\vert$ and $\vert V_{GS}\vert < \vert V_P\vert$ — behaves as a voltage-controlled resistor between its drain and source terminals. The channel resistance is:

$$r_{DS}(V_{GS}) \;\approx\; \frac{r_{DS,on}}{1 - V_{GS}/V_P}$$

where $V_P$ is the **pinch-off voltage** (a negative quantity, typically **−2 V to −8 V** for popular JFETs like 2N3819 and BF245). As $V_{GS}$ approaches 0 V, $r_{DS}$ falls to its minimum (channel fully open), typically a few hundred ohms. As $V_{GS}$ approaches $V_P$, the channel pinches off and $r_{DS} \to \infty$.

When this JFET is placed in the feedback path of an inverting op-amp (replacing the conventional feedback resistor $R_F$), the closed-loop gain becomes:

$$A_v(V_{GS}) \;=\; -\frac{R_F}{r_{DS}(V_{GS})}$$

By varying the gate bias $V_{GS}$ (the control voltage $V_C$), the gain can be smoothly tuned over a range of **40 dB to 60 dB**.

**Pinch-off voltage $V_P$** sets the maximum negative gate bias at which the channel is fully depleted. The usable control range is approximately $0 \geq V_{GS} \geq 0.8\,V_P$ (operating up to 80% of $V_P$ to avoid non-linearity). For a 2N3819 with $V_P = −3.5$ V, this means $V_{GS}$ is restricted to the range **0 V to −2.8 V**, giving an $r_{DS}$ dynamic range of roughly **200 Ω to 50 kΩ**.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_F = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $r_{DS} \in [200\text{ }\Omega, 20\text{ k}\Omega]$, $V_{REF} = 1\text{ V}$.

**(i) Maximum and minimum closed-loop gains:**

The gain magnitude is $A_v = R_F / r_{DS}$.

$$A_{v,max} \;=\; \frac{R_F}{r_{DS,min}} \;=\; \frac{10\text{ k}\Omega}{200\text{ }\Omega} \;=\; 50 \quad (\text{at } V_C = 0\text{ V})$$

$$A_{v,min} \;=\; \frac{R_F}{r_{DS,max}} \;=\; \frac{10\text{ k}\Omega}{20\text{ k}\Omega} \;=\; 0.5 \quad (\text{at } V_C = -4\text{ V})$$

**(ii) Range of input amplitudes for which AGC maintains $V_{out,peak} = 1\text{ V}$:**

Using $V_{out,peak} = A_v \cdot V_{in,peak}$ and $V_{out,peak} = V_{REF} = 1\text{ V}$:

$$V_{in,peak} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{A_v} \;=\; \frac{1\text{ V}}{A_v}$$

$$V_{in,peak,min} \;=\; \frac{1\text{ V}}{A_{v,max}} \;=\; \frac{1}{50} \;=\; 0.02\text{ V} \;=\; 20\text{ mV}$$

$$V_{in,peak,max} \;=\; \frac{1\text{ V}}{A_{v,min}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.5} \;=\; 2.0\text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{in,peak} \;\in\; [20\text{ mV},\, 2.0\text{ V}]\;}$$

**Compression dynamic range:**

$$DR \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in,max}}{V_{in,min}}\right) \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.0}{0.02}\right) \;=\; 20\log_{10}(100) \;=\; 40\text{ dB}$$

This 40 dB dynamic range is more than sufficient to handle the ECG amplitude variability from neonate to adult.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the AGC Question:**
> 1. **Confusing "Automatic Gain Compensation" with "Automatic Volume Control"** in audio — examiners deduct **1 mark** if you fail to mention *closed-loop feedback* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** of $r_{DS}$ (Ω) and $V_{REF}$ (V) in numerical problems — this costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing the integrator sign incorrectly** — the negative sign in $V_C = -(1/R_iC_i)\int V_{\varepsilon}\,dt$ is crucial for **negative feedback**. Reversing it creates positive feedback, and the loop becomes unstable (oscillator). Examiners watch for this and deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing "closed-loop gain" with "open-loop gain"** — the closed-loop AGC gain is $V_{REF}/V_{in,peak}$ (variable), not a fixed $R_F/r_{DS}$.
> 5. **Not stating the steady-state assumption** $dV_C/dt = 0$ explicitly in derivations — examiners look for this word "steady-state" and penalize its absence with **0.5 mark**.
> 6. **Missing the role of the JFET's pinch-off voltage $V_P$** when describing the VCR mechanism — this is a high-yield 1-mark point.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
