<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCBML508]**
**Define an infusion pump. Differentiate between a volumetric infusion pump and a syringe pump with respect to flow rate range, accuracy, and typical clinical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An infusion pump is a microprocessor-controlled medical device that delivers fluids into a patient's body in controlled, programmable volumes.

| Feature | Volumetric Infusion Pump | Syringe Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Peristaltic (linear or rotary) | Direct plunger displacement |
| Flow rate range | 1 – 9999 mL/h | 0.1 – 1200 mL/h |
| Accuracy | ± 5% | ± 2% (higher) |
| Reservoir | IV fluid bag (large) | Disposable syringe (1–60 mL) |
| Typical use | Hydration, electrolytes, blood | Critical drugs, neonates, anaesthesia |

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCBML508]**
**List any four safety alarms built into a modern syringe pump. State the physical principle behind each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Occlusion alarm** — uses a *strain-gauge force sensor* on the plunger pusher; alarms when line pressure > 300 mmHg.
2. **Air-in-line alarm** — uses an *ultrasonic transmitter-receiver pair* across the IV tubing; alarms on detecting an air bubble > 0.05 mL.
3. **Low-battery alarm** — uses *ADC voltage monitoring*; alarms when battery drops below 10.8 V.
4. **End-of-infusion alarm** — uses a *mechanical limit switch* on the carriage; triggers KVO (Keep Vein Open) mode at 0.1 mL/h.

**(½ mark per correct alarm with principle = 3 marks.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, PCBML508 Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a microprocessor-based syringe pump. Label the **sensing, control, actuation, and display** blocks clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 mL syringe has an inner barrel diameter of **25 mm**. The clinician has prescribed a flow rate of **120 mL/h**. Calculate:
   (i) the plunger linear velocity in **mm/h**,
   (ii) the required **motor RPM** if the lead screw pitch is **2 mm/rev**,
   (iii) the total infusion time in **hours** to empty the syringe. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

A syringe pump consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Input block (1 mark)**: Membrane keypad / rotary encoder — clinician enters the prescribed flow rate $Q$ (mL/h) and the total volume to be infused (VTBI in mL).
2. **Microcontroller block (2 marks)**: An 8-bit or 32-bit microcontroller (PIC 18F / STM32 / ARM Cortex-M0). It:
   - Computes the required plunger velocity using $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$.
   - Generates PWM pulses to the motor driver to maintain exact step frequency.
   - Polls sensor inputs every 10–50 ms.
3. **Actuation block (2 marks)**: Stepper motor (typically NEMA 17, 200 steps/rev) coupled via flexible bellows coupler to a lead screw (pitch 0.5–2 mm). Motor speed $N$ satisfies $v = N \cdot p$. The nut drives a linear carriage which pushes the syringe plunger.
4. **Sensing block (1 mark)**: Force sensor on pusher (occlusion), ultrasonic bubble detector on IV line, optical drop sensor on drip chamber.
5. **Display & alarm block (1 mark)**: 16×2 LCD / OLED for flow rate, volume infused, battery status; buzzer + LED array for audio-visual alarms.

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

Given: $D = 25$ mm, $Q = 120$ mL/h, $p = 2$ mm/rev, $V_{syringe} = 50$ mL.

**(i) Plunger velocity (3 marks):**

$$
v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}
$$

Convert $Q$: $Q = 120 \text{ mL/h} = 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 / \text{h}$.

$$
v = \frac{4 \times 120{,}000}{\pi \times (25)^2}
$$

$$
v = \frac{480{,}000}{1963.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{v \approx 244.46 \text{ mm/h}}
$$

**(ii) Motor RPM (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{v}{p} = \frac{244.46}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 122.23 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**(iii) Total infusion time (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{V_{syringe}}{Q} = \frac{50 \text{ mL}}{120 \text{ mL/h}}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 0.4167 \text{ h} = 25 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, PCBML508 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **lead-screw mechanism** used in syringe pumps. Derive the relationship between **plunger linear velocity, motor RPM, and lead-screw pitch**. Why is a lead screw preferred over a rack-and-pinion for this application? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of the **occlusion detection subsystem** and the **air-in-line detector** in a syringe pump. What alarm thresholds are typically used? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Lead-screw mechanism (7 marks):**

A **lead screw** is a threaded rod (helical ridge of uniform pitch) coupled directly to the stepper motor shaft. A matching nut is fixed to the plunger carriage. When the motor rotates by one revolution, the nut translates linearly by exactly one pitch length $p$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

If the motor rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear distance traversed per minute is:

$$
L_{per min} = N \cdot p \quad \text{(mm/min)}
$$

Since $1$ min $= 1/60$ h, the linear velocity in mm/h is:

$$
v_{mm/h} = 60 \cdot N \cdot p
$$

Equating with the volumetric continuity equation $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$:

$$
60 N p = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}
$$

$$
N = \frac{4Q}{60 \pi D^2 p}
$$

**Why lead screw over rack-and-pinion? (2 marks)**

- **Self-locking** — the helical thread prevents back-driving, so the plunger cannot move if the motor stops (critical for patient safety).
- **High mechanical advantage** — small motor torque can generate large thrust forces (needed during occlusion).
- **Compactness** — full linear motion achieved within the syringe barrel length.
- **Predictable resolution** — pitch × steps/rev gives exact volumetric calibration.

**(b) Occlusion and air-in-line detection (7 marks):**

**Occlusion Detection (3.5 marks):**

- A **strain-gauge force sensor** is mounted between the motor's lead-screw nut and the plunger pusher.
- When the IV line is blocked, fluid pressure rises, and the force required to push the plunger increases.
- The sensor's output is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier (AD620 / INA128)** and fed to the microcontroller's ADC.
- The microcontroller computes $P = F / A$ in mmHg.
- **Alarm threshold**: typically **300–900 mmHg** (adjustable per clinical need).
- On threshold breach, the motor is halted and an audible + visual alarm is triggered.

**Air-in-line Detection (3.5 marks):**

- An **ultrasonic transmitter-receiver pair** is clamped across the IV tubing.
- A 40 kHz burst is transmitted; the received signal amplitude is high when fluid (continuous medium) is present and drops sharply when an **air bubble** passes through.
- A photodiode-based **optical detector** is an alternative — it detects changes in light scattering when a bubble crosses the optical window.
- **Alarm threshold**: bubble volume > 0.05 mL (varies by manufacturer).
- On detection, the pump **pauses** infusion until the bubble is cleared (manual override required).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Students write plunger velocity in mm/min instead of mm/h, or forget to convert mL to mm³ in the area–velocity calculation. *Always* state the unit conversions explicitly in your solution.
> 2. **Using the wrong diameter (–1 mark):** Always use the **inner barrel diameter $D$**, not the outer flange diameter or the plunger rod diameter.
> 3. **Confusing volumetric vs. syringe pump (–1 mark):** The two have *different* flow rate ranges and *different* mechanisms (peristaltic vs. positive displacement). Mixing them up in a comparison question costs you full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the alarm threshold value (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically look for numerical threshold values (e.g., 300 mmHg, 0.05 mL bubble) — mentioning only the principle without numbers loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the KVO mode in end-of-infusion (–1 mark):** A complete answer must mention that the pump does *not* fully stop; it switches to *Keep Vein Open* at ~0.1 mL/h to prevent IV line clotting.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition** — Syringe pump is a *positive-displacement* device for *small, high-precision* volumes.
- **Core formula** — $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$ is the **anchor equation** for the entire experiment.
- **Motor type** — Stepper (preferred) over DC, because of open-loop positional accuracy.
- **Lead screw** — Converts rotary to linear; provides self-locking (safety) and high resolution.
- **Sensors** — Force (occlusion), Ultrasonic (bubble), Optical (drop) — *remember all three*.
- **Alarms** — Occlusion (300–900 mmHg), air-in-line (0.05 mL), low-battery (10.8 V), end-of-infusion (→ KVO mode).
- **Accuracy** — Syringe pumps: ± 2% (better than volumetric pumps at ± 5%).
- **Flow range** — 0.1 mL/h to 1200 mL/h; total volume = 1 mL to 60 mL syringe.
- **Standards** — IEC 60601-2-24, ISO 13485 — *cite these for full marks in design-based questions*.
- **Clinical uses** — ICU vasopressors, neonatology, oncology, TIVA, insulin (CSII).
- **Time calculation** — $T = V_{total} / Q$ is the second most-asked formula.
- **Microstepping** — Improves step resolution (e.g., 1/8 mode = 1600 steps/rev for a 200-step motor).
- **Backup feature** — KVO (Keep Vein Open) at 0.1 mL/h post-end-of-infusion.
- **Differentiator** — Syringe pump = *plunger-based*; Volumetric pump = *peristaltic-based*.
- **Force-pressure link** — $P = F / A$ → convert Pa to mmHg (÷ 133.322) for clinical threshold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Simulators and Analysers - Electrical Safety Analyser, ECG Simulator, and Defibrillator Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML508 - CLINICAL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Simulators and Analysers - Electrical Safety Analyser, ECG Simulator, and Defibrillator Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Three Pillars of Clinical Equipment Verification

In any hospital biomedical engineering department, before a medical device is connected to a patient, it must pass a rigorous series of non-clinical verification tests. The three instruments that form the backbone of this verification are:

### A. Electrical Safety Analyser (ESA)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal Definition:** An Electrical Safety Analyser is a precision test instrument that quantifies the electrical hazards associated with a medical device, including protective earth resistance, insulation integrity (dielectric withstand), and all classes of leakage current, in accordance with the international medical electrical equipment safety standard **IEC 60601-1** (and the in-service test standard **IEC 62353**).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Imagine you buy a new phone charger. Before you plug it in, you want to be sure that if a wire inside accidentally comes loose and touches the metal outer shell, the electricity will safely flow to the ground pin in your wall socket instead of into your hand. The ESA is the "safety inspector" that performs exactly this check on life-critical medical devices that are attached to vulnerable patients who may have catheters, electrodes, or even an exposed heart.

### B. ECG Simulator

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal Definition:** An ECG Simulator is a precision signal generator that synthesizes physiologically accurate electrocardiographic waveforms (normal sinus rhythm and a library of arrhythmias) along with the standard calibration pulses, used for the performance verification, acceptance testing, and preventive maintenance of ECG machines, patient monitors, and telemetry systems, conforming to the **ANSI/AAMI EC13** cardiac monitor standard.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of an ECG Simulator as a "stand-in patient" for an ECG machine. Instead of attaching sticky electrodes to a real human chest, you plug the simulator into the ECG machine, and it tells the machine, "Pretend the patient's heart is beating at 75 beats per minute in a normal pattern," or "Now pretend the patient is having a lethal ventricular fibrillation." This lets the technician confirm the monitor is reading hearts correctly without needing a real patient to be sick.

### C. Defibrillator Analyser

> [! [!IMPORTANT]]  
> **Formal Definition:** A Defibrillator Analyser is a specialized load and measurement system, typically built around a calibrated **50 Ω non-inductive resistive load** (simulating a human thoracic impedance), that captures, digitizes, and computes the energy, voltage, current, charge time, and synchronization timing of a defibrillator pulse, in accordance with **IEC 60601-2-4** and **AAMI DF80**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** A defibrillator delivers a massive, life-saving electric shock (up to 360 Joules — enough energy to briefly stop a heart arrhythmia). Before you trust that shock to save a life, you need to know the device is actually delivering the energy the dial says it is. The Defibrillator Analyser acts like a "smart dummy patient" that catches the shock, measures exactly how much energy hit it, and reports back the truth, just like a scale measures your weight.

---

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PCBML508 (Clinical Instrumentation Lab), Experiment 2 focuses on the **operational familiarity, connection procedure, parameter measurement, and result interpretation** of these three instruments. Students are expected to record measured values, compare against safety/action limits, and report pass/fail status.

## 1.2 The Unified Verification Philosophy

All three analysers/simulators share a common engineering philosophy — they are **closed-loop calibration tools** that:

1. **Stimulate** a medical device with a known reference (the simulator), OR
2. **Measure** the response of a medical device to a known stimulus (the analyser), OR
3. **Quantify** the hazardous output parameters of a medical device (the safety analyser).

The cornerstone metric of medical equipment quality is the **standard test load** — for defibrillators this is a rigid **50 Ω** non-reactive resistor, and for ECGs, the input impedance of the test simulator matches the electrode-skin interface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Electrical Safety Analyser — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Class of Equipment & Applied Part Classification

Before any measurement, the operator must declare the device under test (DUT) classification:

| Classification | Description | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Has a protective earth conductor in the power cord; relies on grounding for safety. | (No patient symbol, uses earth) |
| **Class II** | Double-insulated; no protective earth, relies on reinforced insulation. | ⬛⬛ (Double square) |
| **Type B** | Applied part (patient connection) provides standard protection. | 🅱 |
| **Type BF** | Applied part is "Body Floating" — isolated from earth; higher isolation. | 🅱 within square |
| **Type CF** | Applied part is "Cardiac Floating" — direct cardiac connection (e.g., pacing leads); highest isolation. | 🅲 within heart |

### 2.1.2 The Four Mandatory Safety Tests

#### Test 1 — Protective Earth Resistance (Ground Bond Test)

A high test current (typically **25 A AC** or 200 mA for low-current units) is forced between the protective earth pin of the mains plug and the exposed conductive part of the enclosure. The voltage drop is measured and resistance computed.

- **Why:** Verifies the integrity of the safety earth path. A high resistance means a fault current cannot safely clear a fuse or breaker.
- **Limit (IEC 60601-1):** $\le \mathbf{0.1\ \Omega}$ for Class I equipment with detachable power cord; $\le \mathbf{0.2\ \Omega}$ otherwise.
- **Limit (IEC 62353):** $\le \mathbf{0.3\ \Omega}$ for routine in-service testing.

#### Test 2 — Insulation Resistance

A high DC voltage (commonly **500 V DC**) is applied between the mains live/neutral shorted together and the protective earth. The leakage current is measured and converted to resistance.

- **Why:** Confirms the dielectric barrier between the mains conductors and the user/patient-accessible surfaces has not degraded.
- **Limit:** $\ge \mathbf{200\ M\Omega}$ for Class I; $\ge \mathbf{7\ M\Omega}$ for Class II (IEC 60601-1, before 4th edition transition; modern 60601-1 uses dielectric withstand test instead).

#### Test 3 — Dielectric (Hipot) Withstand Test

A high AC voltage (typically **1500 V AC** for mains-to-earth) is applied for one minute to stress the insulation without breakdown.

- **Why:** Catches weak insulation that would pass a low-voltage test but fail under transient overvoltages (lightning, switching surges).
- **Pass criterion:** No breakdown, no arcing, leakage current $\le$ specified threshold.

#### Test 4 — Leakage Current (The Most Critical Test)

Leakage current is the **unintended current that flows through a human body** from the medical device. IEC 60601-1 defines several paths:

| Leakage Current | Path Description |
|---|---|
| **Earth Leakage Current (ILE)** | Flows through the protective earth conductor to ground. |
| **Touch (Enclosure) Leakage Current (IENC)** | Flows from the enclosure/accessible part through an external body to earth (e.g., a nurse touching the device). |
| **Patient Leakage Current (IPLC)** | Flows from an applied part through the patient to earth. |
| **Patient Auxiliary Current (IPAC)** | Flows between two applied parts (e.g., between two ECG electrodes) — does not flow to earth, but can cause microshock. |
| **Mains on Applied Part (MOP)** | Stress test — simulates a high mains voltage appearing on the patient connection. |

For each, the measurement is performed under:
- **NC** = Normal Condition (no faults)
- **SFC** = Single Fault Condition (e.g., earth open, live open, neutral open)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Concept — Microshock vs. Macroshock:** A current of just **10 µA (0.01 mA)** applied directly to the heart (Type CF application) can induce ventricular fibrillation. In contrast, 1 mA is the perception threshold for skin contact. This is why Type CF leakage limits are **10× stricter** than Type B.

## 2.2 ECG Simulator — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Internal Architecture

A modern ECG simulator is a **microcontroller-based arbitrary waveform generator** with a digital-to-analog converter (DAC) output stage. Its memory stores a library of pre-digitized, sample-by-sample ECG waveforms captured from clinical recordings.

**Core blocks:**

1. **Waveform Library (ROM/Flash):** Stores arrays of amplitude samples (typically at 1000 Hz sample rate) for normal sinus rhythm (NSR), 30+ arrhythmias, and calibration pulses.
2. **Rate Selector:** Software stretches/compresses the waveform in time to simulate the desired heart rate (typically **30 to 300 BPM**).
3. **Lead Selector:** Einthoven / Goldberger / Wilson resistor network digitally synthesized; outputs 12 standard leads simultaneously.
3. **Output Attenuator & Buffer:** Precision resistor divider sets output to clinical standard **1 mV = 1 cm deflection** (with 10 mm/mV gain on the monitor).
4. **Calibration Modes:** Square wave (1 mV, 1 Hz), triangle wave, sine wave (for frequency response test).

### 2.2.2 The Standard Test Waveforms per AAMI EC13

| Waveform | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR)** at 60, 75, 100, 120, 150, 200, 250, 300 BPM | Verifies rate accuracy and QRS detection. |
| **Triangle Pulse** (1 mV peak, 100 ms base) | Tests amplitude calibration. |
| **Square Wave** (1 mV, 10 Hz) | Tests paper speed/time base; also high-frequency response. |
| **Sine Wave** at 0.5, 5, 10, 25, 40, 50, 60, 100, 150 Hz | Frequency response characterization. |
| **Arrhythmias:** V-fib, V-tach, A-fib, Asystole, PEA, PVCs, bigeminy, trigeminy, S-T elevation, R-on-T | Tests alarm algorithms and arrhythmia detection. |

## 2.3 Defibrillator Analyser — Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 The Standard 50 Ω Test Load

The human thorax is electrically modeled as a resistive impedance of approximately **25–150 Ω** during a defibrillation shock. The international standard fixes the test load at exactly **50 Ω ± 1%**, non-inductive (to prevent inductive overshoot distorting the measurement).

### 2.2.2 Energy Calculation — The Core Measurement

A defibrillator stores energy $E$ in a capacitor $C$ charged to a voltage $V_c$. When discharged through load $R$, the instantaneous power dissipated is:

$$P(t) = \frac{V(t)^2}{R}$$

The total delivered energy is the integral of power over the discharge time $T$:

$$E = \int_0^T \frac{V(t)^2}{R}\, dt$$

For an ideal **RC discharge** through the 50 Ω load (plus internal resistance), the voltage decays exponentially:

$$V(t) = V_0\, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant. The energy delivered at the end of an indefinitely long discharge would be:

$$E_\infty = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{V_0^2}{2R}\ \text{(when } R \cdot C = \tau \text{ matches)}$$

But because the defibrillator pulse is **truncated** at some width $T$, the actual delivered energy is less. The analyser integrates $V^2(t)/R$ only for the duration the paddle current is flowing.

### 2.3.3 Tilt Measurement

The tilt of an exponential pulse is the percentage of voltage drop from peak to end:

$$\% \text{Tilt} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}} - V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{peak}}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Monophasic Truncated Exponential (MTE):** Tilt typically 30–70% — the pulse is cut off before complete decay.  
> **Biphasic:** Two half-pulses with reversed polarity; the second phase typically has lower tilt and re-energizes the first-phase residual membrane charge to improve efficacy at lower energy.

### 2.3.4 Synchronization (Sync) Test

In synchronized cardioversion, the defibrillator must deliver the shock on the **R wave** of the ECG, not on the vulnerable T wave. The analyser triggers an NSR waveform on the simulator input of the defibrillator, then measures the **sync delay** — the time from the R-wave peak to the shock delivery. IEC 60601-2-4 limit: **$\le$ 60 ms**.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA / CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions for this experiment.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = IR$ | V, A, Ω | Universal |
| 2 | Power Dissipated in Load | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$ | W | Universal |
| 3 | Energy Delivered (truncated pulse) | $E = \displaystyle\int_0^T \dfrac{V(t)^2}{R}\, dt$ | J | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 4 | Stored Capacitor Energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Universal |
| 5 | RC Decay | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ | V | Universal |
| 6 | Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | s | Universal |
| 7 | Pulse Tilt | $\text{Tilt}\,\% = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}} - V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{peak}}} \times 100$ | % | Monophasic |
| 8 | Heart Rate from R-R interval | $\text{BPM} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | beats/min | ECG |
| 9 | ECG Paper Speed / Time | $t = \dfrac{\text{distance (mm)}}{25}$ | s | (25 mm/s) |
| 10 | Amplitude Calibration | $1\,\text{mV} = 10\,\text{mm deflection}$ | mV, mm | AAMI EC13 |
| 11 | Ground Bond Resistance | $R_g = \dfrac{V_{\text{drop}}}{I_{\text{test}}}$ | Ω | $\le 0.1\,\Omega$ (60601-1) |
| 12 | Insulation Resistance | $R_{\text{ins}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{test}}}{I_{\text{leak}}}$ | MΩ | $\ge 200\,\text{M}\Omega$ (Class I) |
| 13 | Earth Leakage Limit (Type B, NC) | $I_{LE} \le 0.5$ | mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 14 | Earth Leakage Limit (Type B, SFC) | $I_{LE} \le 1.0$ | mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 15 | Patient Leakage Limit (Type CF, NC) | $I_{PLC} \le 0.01$ | mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 16 | Patient Leakage Limit (Type CF, SFC) | $I_{PLC} \le 0.05$ | mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 17 | Sync Delay Limit | $t_{\text{delay}} \le 60$ | ms | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 18 | Delivered Energy Tolerance | $\vert \Delta E \vert \le \max(2\,\text{J},\ 15\%)$ | J, % | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 19 | Energy in Biphasic | $E_{\text{total}} = E_1 + E_2$ | J | — |
| 20 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | $\ge 100$ dB |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Engineering Departments** run these tests on every device before it touches a patient (incoming inspection) and annually (preventive maintenance).
- **Accreditation** bodies (NABH, JCI) mandate documented records of these tests.
- **Litigation Protection:** Documented safety tests shield the hospital in case of a patient injury claim.
- **Defibrillator PM Programs** are legally required to verify delivered energy ±15% — this is exactly the analyser test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Delivered in a Truncated Exponential Defibrillator Pulse

> **Problem Statement:** A monophasic truncated exponential defibrillator is charged to a capacitor voltage $V_0 = 2000\,\text{V}$. The capacitor value is $C = 32\,\mu\text{F}$. The pulse is delivered through the standard **50 Ω test load** and is truncated at a pulse width of $T = 4\,\text{ms}$. Compute:
> 1. The stored energy.
> 2. The peak current at $t = 0$.
> 3. The voltage at the end of the pulse ($t = T$).
> 4. The pulse tilt.
> 5. The delivered energy (integrated).
> 6. Compare with the defibrillator dial setting of **200 J**.

---

### Step 1 — Stored Energy in the Capacitor

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times (32 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2000)^2$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 4\,000\,000$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = 64\,\text{J}$$

> The capacitor only stores 64 J — much less than 200 J. This is because real defibrillators either use larger capacitors or charge to higher voltages. Let us scale up: we will set $V_0 = 3535.5\,\text{V}$ (to be solved) to deliver 200 J stored. For this derivation, let us **adjust $V_0$** such that stored ≈ 200 J.

**Adjusted $V_0$ to store 200 J:**

$$V_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{stored}}}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V_0 = \sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{7}} = 3535.53\,\text{V}$$

**Use $V_0 = 3535.5\,\text{V}$ for the remaining steps.**

---

### Step 2 — Time Constant of the Discharge

The load is $R = 50\,\Omega$. The time constant is:

$$\tau = R C = 50 \times (32 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\tau = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1.6\,\text{ms}$$

---

### Step 3 — Peak Current at $t = 0$

By Ohm's law, the initial current is:

$$I_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{3535.5}{50}$$

$$I_{\text{peak}} = 70.71\,\text{A}$$

---

### Step 4 — Voltage at End of Pulse ($t = T = 4\,\text{ms}$)

The exponential discharge equation is:

$$V(T) = V_0 \cdot e^{-T/\tau}$$

$$\frac{T}{\tau} = \frac{4\,\text{ms}}{1.6\,\text{ms}} = 2.5$$

$$V(T) = 3535.5 \times e^{-2.5}$$

$$e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$

$$V(T) = 3535.5 \times 0.0821 = 290.3\,\text{V}$$

---

### Step 5 — Pulse Tilt (Monophasic)

$$\%\text{Tilt} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}} - V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{peak}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Tilt} = \frac{3535.5 - 290.3}{3535.5} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Tilt} = \frac{3245.2}{3535.5} \times 100 = 91.79\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> A 92% tilt is unusually high — typical MTE defibrillators have 30–70% tilt. The pulse is "overly long." If we instead truncate at $T = 2\,\text{ms}$ (i.e., $T/\tau = 1.25$), tilt becomes $\approx 71\%$, which is clinically reasonable.

---

### Step 6 — Delivered Energy (Analytic Closed Form)

For an RC discharge, the delivered energy from $t=0$ to $t=T$ is:

$$E_{\text{del}} = \int_0^T \frac{V(t)^2}{R}\, dt = \int_0^T \frac{V_0^2 e^{-2t/RC}}{R}\, dt$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \int_0^T e^{-2t/RC}\, dt$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \left[ -\frac{RC}{2} e^{-2t/RC} \right]_0^T$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = \frac{V_0^2 \cdot C}{2} \left[ 1 - e^{-2T/RC} \right]$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 \left[ 1 - e^{-2T/\tau} \right]$$

Substituting $E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = 200\,\text{J}$ and $2T/\tau = 5.0$:

$$E_{\text{del}} = 200 \times \left[ 1 - e^{-5.0} \right]$$

$$e^{-5} = 0.00674$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = 200 \times (1 - 0.00674) = 200 \times 0.9933 = 198.65\,\text{J}$$

---

### Step 7 — Compliance Check Against IEC 60601-2-4

The standard tolerance is: $|\Delta E| \le \max(2\,\text{J},\, 15\%)$.

$$\max(2,\ 30) = 30\,\text{J} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{limit is } \pm 30\,\text{J}$$

$$\text{Error} = \vert 200 - 198.65 \vert = 1.35\,\text{J} \le 2\,\text{J} \quad\checkmark$$

> The defibrillator passes the energy delivery test.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of ESA Readings

| Reading | Computed/Measured | Standard Limit (Type B, NC) | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Earth Resistance ($R_g$) | 0.07 Ω | $\le 0.1\,\Omega$ | ✓ PASS |
| Insulation Resistance | 240 MΩ | $\ge 200\,\text{M}\Omega$ | ✓ PASS |
| Earth Leakage ($I_{LE}$) | 0.32 mA | $\le 0.5\,\text{mA}$ | ✓ PASS |
| Touch Leakage ($I_{ENC}$) | 0.05 mA | $\le 0.1\,\text{mA}$ | ✓ PASS |
| Patient Leakage ($I_{PLC}$, Type CF) | 0.008 mA | $\le 0.01\,\text{mA}$ | ✓ PASS |
| Patient Auxiliary ($I_{PAC}$) | 0.015 mA | $\le 0.1\,\text{mA}$ | ✓ PASS |

**Conclusion:** Device complies with IEC 60601-1 for Type BF/CF applications. Cleared for clinical use.

## 3.3 ECG Simulator — Heart Rate / Time-Base Conversion

> **Problem:** An ECG machine is fed a 60 BPM normal sinus rhythm from the simulator. The patient monitor's R-R interval reads 25 mm on the printout. Verify the rate.

**Solution:**

The standard ECG paper speed is $v = 25\,\text{mm/s}$.

$$T_{RR} = \frac{\text{distance}}{v} = \frac{25}{25} = 1.0\,\text{s}$$

$$\text{BPM} = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} = \frac{60}{1.0} = 60\,\text{beats/min}\quad\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]  
> A 1500-ms R-R interval = 40 BPM. A 600-ms R-R interval = 100 BPM. Memorize: 1 large box (5 mm) on standard ECG paper = 0.20 s; 5 large boxes = 1.00 s.

## 3.4 ECG Frequency Response Calculation

> **Problem:** A 10 Hz, 1 mV sine wave from the simulator is applied to an ECG machine. The displayed amplitude is 0.9 mV. Calculate the gain in dB at 10 Hz.

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.9}{1.0}\right)$$

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 20 \times (-0.0458) = -0.916\,\text{dB}$$

> AAMI EC13 allows $\pm 10\%$ amplitude error, i.e., $-0.92\,\text{dB}$ to $+0.83\,\text{dB}$. The reading is within spec.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Electrical Safety Analyser Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AC["AC Mains Source (Isolated Transformer)"]
    PWR["Mains Input Selector (Phase/Neutral/Earth Polarity Reversal)"]
    MCU["Microcontroller / Keypad Control"]
    GB["Ground Bond Current Source (25 A AC)"]
    HV["High Voltage DC Source (500 V) for Insulation"]
    HI["High Voltage AC Source (1500 V) for Hipot"]
    LC["Leakage Current Measurement Network (Body Model: 1 kΩ || 0.015 µF)"]
    DUT["Device Under Test"]
    DSP["Signal Conditioner + ADC"]
    LCD["LCD / Printer Output"]
    MEM["Non-Volatile Memory (Test Records)"]
    PC["PC Interface RS232 / USB"]
    AC --> PWR
    PWR --> DUT
    MCU --> GB
    MCU --> HV
    MCU --> HI
    MCU --> LC
    GB --> DUT
    HV --> DUT
    HI --> DUT
    LC --> DSP
    DSP --> MCU
    MCU --> LCD
    MCU --> MEM
    MCU --> PC
```

**Explanation:** The MCU orchestrates the safety test sequence. The body model network (1 kΩ in parallel with 0.015 µF) is the IEC-standard simulation of human-body impedance for leakage current measurement. The test engineer selects a test (e.g., Earth Leakage NC), the MCU engages the appropriate source, switches the mains polarity to find worst-case, and reads the leakage current via DSP+ADC.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ECG Simulator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KEY["Keypad / Touch Interface"]
    CPU["Microcontroller (ARM/STM32)"]
    ROM["Flash ROM Waveform Library (NSR, Arrhythmias, Calibration)"]
    RATE["Rate Synthesizer (Time-Scaling Algorithm)"]
    DAC["12/16-bit DAC (Update Rate 1 kHz)"]
    ATTEN["Precision Attenuator Network (1 mV, 2 mV, 4 mV ranges)"]
    LEADS["Lead Selector Matrix (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1-V6)"]
    OUT["Output Terminals (Patient Lead Connectors)"]
    PWR["Battery + DC-DC Isolated Supply"]
    KEY --> CPU
    ROM --> CPU
    CPU --> RATE
    RATE --> DAC
    DAC --> ATTEN
    ATTEN --> LEADS
    LEADS --> OUT
    PWR --> CPU
    PWR --> DAC
```

**Explanation:** The user selects "NSR 75 BPM" on the keypad. The CPU fetches the corresponding amplitude-sample array from ROM, the rate synthesizer applies linear time-stretching to set the R-R interval to $60/75 = 0.8\,\text{s}$, the DAC reconstructs the analog waveform at 1 kHz, the attenuator sets the calibration level (1 mV = 1 cm), and the lead matrix outputs the 12 standard lead signals simultaneously through the patient connector.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Defibrillator Analyser Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PADP["Defibrillator Paddle + (Patient Side)"]
    PADM["Defibrillator Paddle - (Patient Side)"]
    LOAD["50 Ω Non-Inductive Precision Load Resistor"]
    VDIV["High-Voltage Divider (1000:1)"]
    CSENSE["Current Sense Resistor (0.01 Ω) or Pearson Coil"]
    ATTEN["Attenuator / Protection Network"]
    ADC["High-Speed ADC (1 MS/s, 12-bit)"]
    MCU["Microcontroller (DSP)"]
    INTEG["Energy Integrator V-squared over R"]
    DISP["LCD Display (Energy, Vpeak, Ipeak, Tilt, Charge Time)"]
    PRT["Thermal Printer (Optional)"]
    SYNC["ECG Input (BNC) for Sync Test"]
    PADP --> LOAD
    PADM --> LOAD
    LOAD --> VDIV
    LOAD --> CSENSE
    VDIV --> ATTEN
    CSENSE --> ATTEN
    ATTEN --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> INTEG
    INTEG --> DISP
    DISP --> PRT
    SYNC --> MCU
```

**Explanation:** When the defibrillator discharges, the high voltage (up to ~3500 V) and current (up to ~70 A) flow through the **50 Ω load**. The voltage divider scales it down by 1000× to within ADC range. The current is sensed via a low-side shunt or Pearson coil. The ADC samples both waveforms at 1 MS/s, the MCU integrates $V^2(t)/R$ to compute delivered energy, and the LCD displays all parameters. In **sync test mode**, the analyser's ECG input watches the defibrillator's R-wave detection, and the timing between R-peak and shock is measured.

---

## 4.4 Test Setup — Complete Clinical Equipment Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ESA["Electrical Safety Analyser"]
    ECGS["ECG Simulator"]
    DEFA["Defibrillator Analyser"]
    DUT1["ECG Machine"]
    DUT2["Defibrillator"]
    DUT3["Patient Monitor / Infusion Pump / etc."]
    DUT1 -->|ECG Cable| ECGS
    DUT1 -->|Mains Plug| ESA
    DUT2 -->|Paddles| DEFA
    DUT2 -->|ECG Input| ECGS
    DUT3 -->|Mains Plug| ESA
    ESA -->|Result| RECORD["Test Record Database"]
    ECGS -->|Verification| RECORD
    DEFA -->|Verification| RECORD
```

**Explanation:** The verification flow in a hospital biomedical lab. Every device entering clinical service is first electrically safety-tested, then for monitors/recorders an ECG simulator verifies the signal chain, and for defibrillators a defibrillator analyser confirms the shock delivery. All results go to a centralized record database (paper or CMMS software) for accreditation audits.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define leakage current. List any four types of leakage currents specified in IEC 60601-1.** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Leakage current** is the **unintended current** that flows from the medical device's conductive parts (enclosure, applied parts, signal connectors) through a person to earth, or between applied parts, in the absence of an intended physiological effect.

The four principal types per IEC 60601-1 are:

1. **Earth Leakage Current ($I_{LE}$):** Current flowing through the protective earth conductor to ground.
2. **Touch (Enclosure) Leakage Current ($I_{ENC}$):** Current flowing from the accessible enclosure to earth through an external body.
3. **Patient Leakage Current ($I_{PLC}$):** Current flowing from an applied part through the patient to earth.
4. **Patient Auxiliary Current ($I_{PAC}$):** Current flowing between two applied parts (e.g., two ECG electrodes) without going to earth.

---

### **Q2. Why is a 50 Ω non-inductive load used in a defibrillator analyser?** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **50 Ω non-inductive resistive load** is used for the following reasons:

1. **It represents the median human thoracic impedance** during a defibrillation shock. International standards (IEC 60601-2-4) have standardized on 50 Ω as the universal test load to ensure consistent and comparable energy measurements across all defibrillator manufacturers.

2. **"Non-inductive" construction** (e.g., wound in a bifilar fashion on a ceramic substrate) prevents the load from storing magnetic energy. If the load were inductive, the $L\,di/dt$ kick at the end of the pulse would distort the measurement and could damage the analyser's input stage.

3. **High power rating and precision** (±1% tolerance) — must dissipate up to 360 J in a few milliseconds without resistance drift from heating.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A: ELECTRICAL SAFETY ANALYSER** [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the different classes of medical electrical equipment (Class I, II) and the types of applied parts (Type B, BF, CF) as per IEC 60601-1. Discuss the leakage current limits for each combination. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Equipment Classification (Class I & Class II):**

- **Class I Equipment:** Has a **functional insulation** between live parts and accessible conductive parts, but safety is provided by an **additional protective earth connection**. If a fault occurs (e.g., live wire touches the metal case), the fault current flows through the protective earth to ground and trips the circuit breaker/fuse. Examples: Patient monitors, defibrillators.

- **Class II Equipment:** Has **double insulation** (functional + supplementary) and **no protective earth**. The casing is either fully insulated plastic or has reinforced insulation. Examples: Some infusion pumps, handheld ultrasound probes.

**(ii) Applied Part Types:**

| Type | Symbol | Application | Isolation Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type B (Body)** | 🅱 | Earth-referenced patient connection; no patient protection against electrical shock other than basic insulation. | Lowest — e.g., X-ray table, hospital bed. |
| **Type BF (Body Floating)** | 🅱 in square | F (floating) applied part; current-limited to prevent microshock. | Medium — e.g., ECG electrodes, NIBP cuff, pulse oximeter. |
| **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)** | 🅲 in heart | Highest isolation; permitted for direct cardiac contact. | Highest — e.g., pacing leads, intracardiac catheters, defibrillator paddles. |

**(iii) Leakage Current Limits (per IEC 60601-1):**

| Leakage Type | B (NC) | B (SFC) | BF (NC) | BF (SFC) | CF (NC) | CF (SFC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth Leakage (mA) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| Touch Leakage (mA) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.1 | 0.5 |
| Patient Leakage (mA) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.1 | 0.5 | **0.01** | **0.05** |
| Patient Auxiliary (mA) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.01 | 0.05 |

> The CF limits are 10× tighter because microshock currents as low as 10 µA can trigger ventricular fibrillation when applied directly to the heart.

---

#### (b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an Electrical Safety Analyser. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**(Block diagram) — Refer Section 4.1 above.**

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Mains Source with Polarity Reversal:** The ESA has an internal isolated AC source that allows automatic reversal of Line/Neutral to find the **worst-case leakage condition**.

2. **Test Signal Sources:**
   - **Ground Bond Source:** A 25 A AC (or 200 mA) constant current is injected through the earth path of the DUT, and the resulting voltage drop is measured to compute $R_g = V/I$.
   - **Insulation Test Source:** A 500 V DC source is applied between mains conductors and earth; the leakage current is measured and converted to $R_{\text{ins}}$.
   - **Hipot Source:** 1500 V AC for dielectric withstand testing.

3. **Body Model Network:** Per IEC 60601-1, the leakage current is measured through a network representing human body impedance — **1 kΩ in parallel with 0.015 µF**. This network models the worst-case skin impedance at mains frequency.

4. **Signal Conditioner & ADC:** The measured microamp-level leakage current is amplified, filtered, and digitized.

5. **MCU Controller:** Sequences the tests, switches relays, inverts mains polarity, evaluates pass/fail against pre-stored limits, and displays results.

6. **Display/Printer/PC Interface:** Outputs the result numerically and as a printed certificate for documentation.

**Typical Measurement Procedure:**

1. Plug DUT into ESA via a special test cable.
2. Select test routine: e.g., "Earth Leakage NC → SFC, Type B."
3. Press RUN. ESA automatically measures under both polarities.
4. Worst-case reading is logged.
5. Compare to IEC 60601-1 limit; display PASS/FAIL.

---

### **Question B: ECG SIMULATOR + DEFIBRILLATOR ANALYSER** [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain with a block diagram the working of an ECG Simulator. List the standard test waveforms used for ECG equipment performance testing. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(Block diagram) — Refer Section 4.2 above.**

**Working Explanation:**

The ECG simulator mimics the electrical output of a human heart with sufficient fidelity to verify an ECG machine end-to-end.

1. **Waveform Library (ROM/Flash):** Stores pre-recorded digitized ECG complexes (NSR, arrhythmias) sampled at typically 1000 Hz with 12-bit resolution. A typical 10-second NSR recording occupies 10,000 samples per lead.

2. **Rate Synthesizer:** A digital time-scaling algorithm stretches/compresses the R-R interval to produce the desired BPM from 30 to 300. Mathematically, the original samples are interpolated (linear or cubic spline) to a new sample count.

3. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC):** The processed digital samples are output at 1 kHz to reconstruct the analog ECG waveform.

4. **Precision Attenuator:** A calibrated resistor divider sets the output amplitude so that **1 mV input produces a 10 mm deflection** on a standard ECG machine (gain = 10 mm/mV).

5. **Lead Matrix:** Uses a digital switch network and the Einthoven/Goldberger/Wilson lead equations to derive all 12 standard ECG leads from a single set of limb and precordial signals.

6. **Output Terminals:** Patient lead connectors (5-lead or 10-lead) match the ECG machine's input.

7. **Calibration Waveform Generation:** Separate routine outputs a 1 mV, 100 ms triangle pulse for amplitude calibration, and a 1 mV, 10 Hz square wave for time-base / paper-speed verification.

**Standard Test Waveforms (per AAMI EC13):**

- **Normal Sinus Rhythm** at 30, 60, 75, 100, 120, 150, 200, 250, 300 BPM
- **Triangle pulse** (1 mV peak, 100 ms) for amplitude calibration
- **Square wave** (1 mV, 10 Hz) for time base
- **Sine waves** at 0.5, 5, 10, 25, 40, 50, 60, 100, 150 Hz for frequency response
- **Arrhythmias:** V-fib, V-tach, A-fib, asystole, PVCs, bigeminy, S-T elevation

---

#### (b) A monophasic truncated exponential defibrillator has $C = 32\,\mu\text{F}$ and is charged to 3000 V. The discharge is delivered through the standard 50 Ω test load and is truncated at $T = 3\,\text{ms}$. Compute: (i) stored energy, (ii) time constant, (iii) peak current, (iv) voltage at end of pulse, (v) pulse tilt, (vi) delivered energy. Compare with the dial setting of 144 J. State pass/fail per IEC 60601-2-4. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Stored Energy:**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (32 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3000)^2$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^{6} = 144\,\text{J}\quad\checkmark$$

**(ii) Time Constant:**

$$\tau = RC = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6\,\text{ms}$$

**(iii) Peak Current:**

$$I_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{3000}{50} = 60\,\text{A}$$

**(iv) Voltage at End of Pulse ($T = 3\,\text{ms}$):**

$$\frac{T}{\tau} = \frac{3}{1.6} = 1.875$$

$$V(T) = V_0 \cdot e^{-T/\tau} = 3000 \times e^{-1.875}$$

$$e^{-1.875} = 0.1534$$

$$V(T) = 3000 \times 0.1534 = 460.2\,\text{V}$$

**(v) Pulse Tilt:**

$$\%\text{Tilt} = \frac{3000 - 460.2}{3000} \times 100 = \frac{2539.8}{3000} \times 100 = 84.66\%$$

**(vi) Delivered Energy (Integrated):**

$$E_{\text{del}} = E_{\text{stored}} \left[ 1 - e^{-2T/\tau} \right]$$

$$\frac{2T}{\tau} = 2 \times 1.875 = 3.75$$

$$e^{-3.75} = 0.02352$$

$$E_{\text{del}} = 144 \times (1 - 0.02352) = 144 \times 0.9765 = 140.6\,\text{J}$$

**Compliance Check (IEC 60601-2-4):**

$$\text{Tolerance} = \max(2\,\text{J},\ 15\%\,\text{of}\,144) = \max(2,\, 21.6) = 21.6\,\text{J}$$

$$\text{Error} = \vert 144 - 140.6 \vert = 3.4\,\text{J} \le 2\,\text{J}\quad\color{red}{\text{FAILS}}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Ktu Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The 2 J absolute minimum applies when the % tolerance would be smaller than 2 J. Here, 15% = 21.6 J > 2 J, so the 15% rule governs. The error 3.4 J is well within 21.6 J. **Therefore the device PASSES.** Many students wrongly compare 3.4 J to 2 J and conclude FAIL — that is incorrect. The tolerance is the **larger** of the two: $\max(2\,\text{J},\ 15\%)$. The correct verdict is **PASS**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Experiment:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IEC 60601-1 vs IEC 62353 limits:** 60601-1 is the *manufacturer* type-test standard (stricter); 62353 is the *in-service* test standard (looser). Use the one relevant to the question.
> 
> 2. **Patient Leakage vs Patient Auxiliary confusion:** Patient leakage flows to **earth**; patient auxiliary flows **between applied parts**. They have different limits.
> 
> 3. **Tilt ≠ Delivered Energy Loss:** Tilt is a *voltage* ratio, not an *energy* ratio. Energy in an exponential pulse is $1 - e^{-2T/\tau}$ (note the **factor of 2** in the exponent — students often forget it).
> 
> 4. **Energy tolerance is "max" not "sum":** $\max(2\,\text{J}, 15\%)$ — you do **not** add them.
> 
> 5. **NSR = Normal Sinus Rhythm, not "normal sinus."** Always write the expansion once in the exam.
> 
> 6. **Paper speed = 25 mm/s, gain = 10 mm/mV** — memorize these AAMI EC13 constants.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Electrical Safety Analyser** performs 4 main tests: **Ground Bond (≤ 0.1 Ω)**, **Insulation (≥ 200 MΩ)**, **Hipot (1500 V AC)**, and **Leakage Current** (multiple paths).
- [ ] Leakage currents are measured through the **1 kΩ || 0.015 µF body model**.
- [ ] **Class I** = earthed; **Class II** = double-insulated. **Type B/BF/CF** = applied-part isolation level.
- [ ] **Type CF Patient Leakage limit = 0.01 mA (NC)** — 10× stricter than Type B because of microshock hazard at 10 µA.
- [ ] Tests run under **NC (Normal Condition)** and **SFC (Single Fault Condition)**.
- [ ] **ECG Simulator** outputs: NSR (30–300 BPM), arrhythmias, calibration triangle/square, and sine waves.
- [ ] Standard calibration: **1 mV = 10 mm deflection**, paper speed **25 mm/s**, time constant **0.2 s per large box**.
- [ ] **Defibrillator Analyser** uses a **50 Ω non-inductive load**.
- [ ] **Stored energy** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
- [ ] **Delivered energy** (truncated exponential) $E_{\text{del}} = E_{\text{stored}} \left[ 1 - e^{-2T/RC} \right]$.
- [ ] **Tilt** is a *voltage* ratio: $\%\text{Tilt} = (V_{\text{peak}} - V_{\text{final}})/V_{\text{peak}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **IEC 60601-2-4** energy tolerance: $\pm \max(2\,\text{J},\, 15\%)$.
- [ ] **Synchronization delay** (sync-mode cardioversion) must be $\le 60\,\text{ms}$ from R-peak.
- [ ] **Biphasic** defibrillators deliver $E_1$ (phase 1) + $E_2$ (phase 2) for a total lower-than-monophasic energy dose.
- [ ] **AAMI EC13** governs ECG performance; **AAMI DF80** / IEC 60601-2-4 governs defibrillators.
- [ ] A defibrillator's pulse **width is measured at 50% peak amplitude**, not at start.
- [ ] **CMRR** for ECG machines must be $\ge 100\,\text{dB}$ per AAMI EC13.
- [ ] **Mains polarity reversal** during leakage testing ensures worst-case capture.
- [ ] Always record the **highest leakage** between normal/reversed polarity — that is the "worst case" used for compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Basic Sensor Circuits to Acquire and Display the Physiological Parameter: Volumetric Measurement using Photoplethysmography or Plethysmography / Monitoring of Body Temperature, Heart Rate and Blood Pressure / Measuring the Oxygen Saturation using Photoplethysmography / Electronic Stethoscope for Characterization of Physiological Acoustic Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML508 - CLINICAL INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Basic Sensor Circuits to Acquire and Display the Physiological Parameter: Volumetric Measurement using Photoplethysmography or Plethysmography / Monitoring of Body Temperature, Heart Rate and Blood Pressure / Measuring the Oxygen Saturation using Photoplethysmography / Electronic Stethoscope for Characterization of Physiological Acoustic Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photoplethysmography (PPG) / Plethysmography

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a non-invasive optical technique used to detect volumetric changes in blood within peripheral vessels (typically the fingertip or earlobe) by measuring the intensity of light transmitted through or reflected from the tissue. The PPG signal provides information about cardiac cycle, blood volume, and vascular health.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a **flashlight behind your finger in the dark**. When your heart pumps, blood rushes into your fingertip, momentarily making it "redder" and absorbing more light. As the heart relaxes, the blood flows out, and the finger becomes slightly more translucent. A PPG sensor essentially counts these tiny light-intensity changes to track your heartbeat — much like watching the rise and fall of a tide to measure the ocean's rhythm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PPG is the foundational technique for measuring **Pulse Rate (BPM)**, **Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂)**, and **Perfusion Index (PI)**. It forms the core of modern wearable health monitors (smartwatches, pulse oximeters).

## 1.2 Body Temperature Monitoring

**Formal Definition:**
Body temperature measurement is the quantitative assessment of the thermal state of the human body using contact sensors (thermistor, RTD, thermocouple) or non-contact sensors (infrared). Normal oral temperature in adults is **36.1 °C to 37.2 °C** (97 °F to 99 °F).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A clinical thermometer works like a **road expansion joint** — metal or semiconductor materials expand (or change resistance) predictably with heat. The hotter you are, the more the sensor's electrical property shifts, which the circuit translates into a readable temperature value on a display.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constants for KTU:**
> - Body temperature range: **32 °C – 42 °C**
> - Resolution required: **0.1 °C**
> - Standard sensor: **NTC Thermistor (10 kΩ at 25 °C)**

## 1.3 Heart Rate Monitoring

**Formal Definition:**
Heart Rate (HR) is the number of cardiac cycles (heartbeats) per minute, expressed in **Beats Per Minute (BPM)**. The normal adult resting heart rate is **60–100 BPM**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a **drummer tapping at a steady rhythm**. Each tap is a heartbeat. If the drummer taps 72 times in 60 seconds, the heart rate is 72 BPM. PPG/ECG systems are essentially "listening" to this drummer using optical or electrical sensors.

## 1.4 Blood Pressure Monitoring

**Formal Definition:**
Blood Pressure (BP) is the lateral pressure exerted by circulating blood on the walls of arteries. It is measured as:
- **Systolic BP (SBP):** Peak pressure during ventricular contraction (~120 mmHg normal).
- **Diastolic BP (DBP):** Minimum pressure during ventricular relaxation (~80 mmHg normal).
- **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP):** $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A **garden hose** with a nozzle helps visualize BP. When you squeeze the trigger (systole), pressure peaks. When you release (diastole), pressure drops. A sphygmomanometer (BP cuff) measures these peaks and troughs using a **pressure transducer** that converts mechanical cuff pressure into a voltage signal.

## 1.5 Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂) Measurement

**Formal Definition:**
SpO₂ (Peripheral Oxygen Saturation) is the ratio of oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂) to total hemoglobin in peripheral blood, expressed as a percentage. Normal SpO₂: **95% – 100%**.

$$
SpO_2 = \frac{[HbO_2]}{[HbO_2] + [Hb]} \times 100\%
$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Hemoglobin is a **taxi service for oxygen**. SpO₂ measures how many taxis are currently occupied vs. empty. 98% saturation means 98 out of 100 taxis are carrying oxygen passengers.

## 1.6 Electronic Stethoscope

**Formal Definition:**
An electronic stethoscope is an acoustical-medical device that converts body sounds (heart, lung, bowel) into electrical signals using a microphone/condenser sensor, amplifies and filters them, and presents the output as an audible signal or a spectrogram (PCG – Phonocardiogram).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A traditional stethoscope is a **passive megaphone** — just a tube carrying sound. An electronic stethoscope is a **smart amplifier** that records the sound, removes noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum), and can even visualize the heart's "lub-dub" on a screen like a **musical score**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PPG Operating Principle — Beer-Lambert Law

The PPG sensor consists of:
1. **Light Source:** LED (Red ~660 nm, Infrared ~940 nm for SpO₂; Green ~530 nm for heart rate in wearables).
2. **Photodetector:** Photodiode or phototransistor.
3. **Tissue Bed:** Fingertip, earlobe, or wrist (arteriole-rich).

### Operational Steps:
- **Step 1:** LED emits light into the tissue.
- **Step 2:** Light is absorbed by **skin, bone, venous blood, and arterial blood**.
- **Step 3:** The **AC component** of the photodetector signal corresponds to pulsatile arterial blood (systole-diastole variation).
- **Step 4:** The **DC component** corresponds to non-pulsatile tissues and venous blood.
- **Step 5:** The signal is amplified, filtered (0.5 Hz – 10 Hz for HR), and displayed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two wavelengths for SpO₂?** HbO₂ and Hb absorb red and IR light **differently**. By taking the ratio of AC/DC at two wavelengths, tissue and bone effects cancel out, isolating oxygen saturation.

## 2.2 Body Temperature Sensor Circuits

### A. NTC Thermistor (Steinhart-Hart Equation)

$$
\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3
$$

Where $T$ is in Kelvin, $R$ is thermistor resistance, and A, B, C are calibration constants.

### B. RTD (PT100) — Linear Approximation

$$
R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]
$$

For PT100: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0\ ^\circ C$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/^\circ C$.

### C. Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect)

$$
V = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S(T)\, dT \approx S\,(T_2 - T_1)
$$

For Type K: $S \approx 41\ \mu V/^\circ C$. Type K is unsuitable for body temp due to low sensitivity — **thermistors are preferred** clinically.

## 2.3 Heart Rate Detection from PPG

The PPG signal is essentially periodic. Heart rate is computed via:

$$
HR\ (BPM) = \frac{60}{T_{peak-to-peak}}
$$

Where $T_{peak-to-peak}$ is the time interval (in seconds) between two consecutive systolic peaks. Algorithmically:
- Detect peaks above a threshold.
- Compute average inter-beat interval (IBI).
- Apply digital filtering (moving average or autocorrelation) to suppress motion artifacts.

## 2.4 Blood Pressure Measurement

### A. Auscultatory Method (Mercury Sphygmomanometer)
- Inflate cuff above SBP → no sound (Korotkoff silent).
- Slowly deflate; first sound = **SBP**, disappearance = **DBP**.

### B. Oscillometric Method (Automated)
- Microprocessor-controlled cuff senses cuff pressure oscillations.
- MAP is identified as the **maximum oscillation amplitude**.
- SBP and DBP are empirically derived at oscillation amplitudes of ~0.5 × MAP and ~0.8 × MAP respectively.

### C. Pressure Transducer Equation

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(0.004 \cdot P - 0.04\right)
$$

Where $P$ is in mmHg. (e.g., for MPX5050 sensor, $V_{ex} = 5\ V$, output 0.2 V – 4.7 V corresponds to 0 – 300 mmHg.)

## 2.5 SpO₂ Ratio-of-Ratios Algorithm

Define for each wavelength $\lambda$:

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{AC_\lambda / DC_\lambda}{AC_{IR} / DC_{IR}}
$$

Then an empirical calibration curve gives:

$$
SpO_2 = A - B \cdot R
$$

Typical values: $A \approx 110$, $B \approx 25$, giving $SpO_2 \approx 97\%$ when $R \approx 0.5$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favorite:** "Why use the ratio-of-ratios instead of a single wavelength?" → It cancels out skin pigmentation, bone, and tissue thickness, since these affect both wavelengths equally.

## 2.6 Electronic Stethoscope Signal Processing

The acoustic sensor (electret condenser or piezoelectric) output is typically in the range of **1 mV – 10 mV**. It undergoes:
1. **Preamplifier** (gain ~100, INA128 or AD620 instrumentation amp).
2. **Bandpass filter** (20 Hz – 1 kHz for heart sounds, 100 Hz – 1.5 kHz for lung sounds).
3. **50 Hz Notch filter** (to remove mains interference).
4. **Power amplifier** (LM386, gain 20–200) for headphone output.
5. **ADC + DSP** (for spectrogram / PCG display).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Equation | Units | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| HR (BPM) | $60 / T_{pp}$ | BPM | 60 – 100 |
| SpO₂ | $[HbO_2]/([HbO_2]+[Hb]) \times 100$ | % | 95 – 100 |
| R-ratio (SpO₂) | $(AC_R/DC_R) / (AC_{IR}/DC_{IR})$ | dimensionless | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| MAP | $DBP + (SBP - DBP)/3$ | mmHg | 70 – 100 |
| Pulse Pressure | $PP = SBP - DBP$ | mmHg | 30 – 50 |
| Steinhart-Hart | $1/T = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | K | — |
| PT100 RTD | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | Ω | 100 Ω @ 0 °C |
| Thermocouple | $V \approx S(T_2 - T_1)$ | V | 41 μV/°C (K-type) |
| Pressure Sensor | $V_{out} = kP + V_{offset}$ | V | 5 V full scale |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, always state **units** and **assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming normal ambient conditions, room temperature 25 °C..."). Marks are deducted for missing units in the KTU valuation key.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PPG-based wearables:** Apple Watch, Fitbit, Masimo pulse oximeters.
- **Continuous BP cuffs:** ICU patient monitors (Philips IntelliVue).
- **Infrared thermometers:** COVID-era screening (MLX90614 sensor).
- **Electronic stethoscopes:** 3M Littmann 3200, Eko Core — used for **telemedicine** and AI-based cardiac diagnostics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law for PPG

When light of intensity $I_0$ passes through a tissue of thickness $d$ containing an absorbing medium with concentration $c$ and molar extinction coefficient $\varepsilon$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is given by:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot d}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the absorbance $A_\lambda$:**

$$
A_\lambda = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot c \cdot d
$$

**Step 2 — For a PPG sensor, decompose the path into static and dynamic components:**

$$
A_\lambda = A_{static} + \Delta A_\lambda(t)
$$

Where $A_{static}$ is due to skin, bone, and venous blood (DC), and $\Delta A_\lambda(t)$ is the pulsatile arterial component (AC).

**Step 3 — Express the transmitted intensity with two components:**

$$
I(t) = I_{DC} - I_{AC}(t)
$$

So the photodetector output has:

$$
V_{photo}(t) = V_{DC} - V_{AC}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — The ratio of AC to DC represents the perfusion index:**

$$
PI = \frac{V_{AC}}{V_{DC}} \times 100\%
$$

Typical PI: **0.02% to 20%**. A low PI indicates poor peripheral perfusion (cold finger, shock).

**Step 5 — Substitute back into Beer-Lambert with two absorbing species (HbO₂ and Hb):**

$$
A_\lambda = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) \cdot c_{HbO_2} \cdot d + \varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) \cdot c_{Hb} \cdot d
$$

This forms the basis of the SpO₂ ratio derivation below.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of SpO₂ from Ratio-of-Ratios

**Step 1 — Express AC and DC for red (R) and infrared (IR) wavelengths:**

At wavelength $\lambda_R$ (red, 660 nm):
$$
\frac{AC_R}{DC_R} = \frac{\Delta A_R}{A_R} = \frac{\Delta d_R}{d_R} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^R \cdot c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}^R \cdot c_{Hb}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^R \cdot c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}^R \cdot c_{Hb}}
$$

For the ratio to be useful, we use the **modulation ratio**:

$$
M_\lambda = \frac{AC_\lambda}{DC_\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio of modulation ratios:**

$$
R = \frac{M_R}{M_{IR}} = \frac{AC_R / DC_R}{AC_{IR} / DC_{IR}}
$$

**Step 3 — Using the extinction coefficient values (standardized in calibration):**
- $\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^R \approx 320\ L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon_{Hb}^R \approx 3200\ L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{IR} \approx 1200\ L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon_{Hb}^{IR} \approx 600\ L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$

Substituting:

$$
R = \frac{a \cdot S + b}{c \cdot S + d}
$$

Where $S = c_{HbO_2} / (c_{HbO_2} + c_{Hb}) = SpO_2 / 100$ and a, b, c, d are constants derived from extinction coefficients.

**Step 4 — Solve for SpO₂:**

$$
SpO_2 = \frac{R \cdot d - b}{a - R \cdot c}
$$

In practice, manufacturers pre-calibrate this with a **lookup table** from healthy volunteers at known SpO₂ levels (verified by arterial blood gas analysis).

**Numerical example (KTU typical question):**
- $M_R = 0.05$, $M_{IR} = 0.10$
- $R = 0.5$
- Using $SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$:

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \times 0.5 = 110 - 12.5 = 97.5\%
$$

This is within the **normal clinical range (95–100%)**.

---

## 3.3 Heart Rate Derivation from PPG Signal

**Step 1 — Sampling:** ADC samples the PPG signal at $f_s$ (e.g., 100 Hz).

**Step 2 — Peak Detection:** Apply a moving threshold:
- Compute the running mean of the signal.
- Set threshold = mean + k × standard deviation (k = 0.5 to 1).
- Detect indices $n_1, n_2, ..., n_N$ where the signal crosses this threshold (peaks).

**Step 3 — Compute Inter-Beat Intervals (IBI):**

$$
IBI_i = \frac{n_{i+1} - n_i}{f_s} \quad (\text{in seconds})
$$

**Step 4 — Average IBI and convert to BPM:**

$$
HR = \frac{60}{\overline{IBI}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s \cdot (N-1)}{n_N - n_1}
$$

**Numerical example:**
- $f_s = 100\ Hz$
- Detected peaks at $n_1 = 200$, $n_2 = 300$ → $IBI_1 = (300-200)/100 = 1\ s$
- $HR = 60/1 = 60\ BPM$ → normal resting heart rate.

---

## 3.4 Pressure Transducer Output Derivation

**Step 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Equation:**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor (e.g., MPX5050) is a four-resistor bridge:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — With applied pressure, resistors change by $\Delta R$:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2R}{R+\Delta R}\right)
$$

For small $\Delta R/R$:

$$
V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}
$$

**Step 3 — The strain-induced resistance change is proportional to pressure:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot P
$$

Where $G$ is the gauge factor (~100 for silicon) and $P$ is pressure.

**Step 4 — Substituting:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G}{2} \cdot P
$$

For MPX5050: $V_{ex} = 5\ V$, $G/2 = 0.004\ V/V/mmHg$:

$$
V_{out} = 5 \times 0.004 \times P = 0.02 \cdot P
$$

So for $P = 120\ mmHg$ (SBP): $V_{out} = 0.02 \times 120 = 2.4\ V$ (plus an offset of ~0.2 V).

---

## 3.5 Body Temperature from NTC Thermistor (Wheatstone Bridge)

**Step 1 — Thermistor resistance (β-parameter equation):**

$$
R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}
$$

For a typical 10 kΩ NTC at 25 °C with $\beta = 3950\ K$:

**Step 2 — Bridge balance equation:**

At balance, $V_{out} = 0$ when $R_T = R_1 \cdot R_3 / R_2$.

**Step 3 — Off-balance output:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\frac{R_T}{R_T + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**
- $T_0 = 298.15\ K$ (25 °C), $R_0 = 10\ k\Omega$, $\beta = 3950\ K$
- $T = 310.15\ K$ (37 °C, body temperature):

$$
R_T = 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)}
$$

$$
= 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \cdot (0.003224 - 0.003354)}
$$

$$
= 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \cdot (-1.30 \times 10^{-4})}
$$

$$
= 10000 \cdot e^{-0.5135} = 10000 \times 0.5986 = 5986\ \Omega \approx 5.99\ k\Omega
$$

This ~40% resistance drop at body temperature is easily detected by the bridge circuit.

---

## 3.6 Electronic Stethoscope Frequency Analysis

**Step 1 — Heart sound frequency content:**
- S1 ("lub"): 20 – 150 Hz
- S2 ("dub"): 25 – 200 Hz
- Murmurs: up to 600 – 1000 Hz

**Step 2 — Lung sound frequency content:**
- Normal breath: 100 – 1000 Hz
- Wheezes: 400 – 1600 Hz
- Crackles: 100 – 2000 Hz

**Step 3 — Filter design (Butterworth 2nd order):**
Low-pass cutoff: 1.5 kHz (for lung), 1 kHz (for heart).
High-pass cutoff: 20 Hz (to remove DC offset and low-frequency motion).

**Step 4 — Total gain calculation:**
- Sensor output: 5 mV
- INA128 preamp gain: 100 → 500 mV
- LM386 power amp gain: 50 → 25 V peak (clipped to 5 V supply) → drives 32 Ω headphone to ~80 dB SPL.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — PPG / SpO₂ Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source LED Red 660nm and IR 940nm] --> B[Fingertip or Earlobe Tissue]
    B --> C[Photodiode or Photodetector]
    C --> D[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    D --> E[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 10 Hz]
    E --> F1[Red Channel AC and DC Extractor]
    E --> F2[IR Channel AC and DC Extractor]
    F1 --> G1[Red AC divided by DC Ratio]
    F2 --> G2[IR AC divided by DC Ratio]
    G1 --> H[Ratio of Ratios Calculator R]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[SpO2 Lookup Table or Empirical Equation]
    I --> J[Microcontroller Display LCD]
    E --> K[Peak Detector for HR]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Heart Rate BPM and SpO2 percent Display]
```

**Explanation:** The dual-wavelength LED alternately illuminates the tissue. The photodiode receives transmitted/reflected light. After TIA conversion, the signal is split into two channels (Red and IR). Each channel's AC and DC components are extracted, and the ratio-of-ratios is computed to derive SpO₂ independently of tissue thickness.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Body Temperature Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Skin Surface] --> B[NTC Thermistor 10k ohm at 25C]
    B --> C[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    C --> D[Differential Amplifier AD620 or INA128]
    D --> E[Low Pass Filter for Noise Removal]
    E --> F[ADC Microcontroller Input]
    F --> G[Linearization via Steinhart Hart Equation]
    G --> H[Digital Display LCD 7 Segment in Degree Celsius]
    H --> I[Optional Buzzer Alarm if T greater than 38C or T less than 35C]
```

**Explanation:** The NTC thermistor forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. The differential voltage proportional to temperature imbalance is amplified, digitized, and converted to temperature using the Steinhart-Hart equation. A threshold alarm can be added for clinical use (e.g., fever detection).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Oscillometric Blood Pressure Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arm Cuff Inflatable] --> B[DC Pump Motor]
    B --> C[Pressure Transducer MPX5050 Piezoresistive]
    C --> D[Differential Amplifier]
    D --> E[ADC Channel 1 for Cuff Pressure]
    D --> F[ADC Channel 2 for Oscillation Amplitude]
    F --> G[Peak Envelope Detector]
    G --> H[Algorithm MAP equals max oscillation]
    H --> I[Empirical SBP and DBP extraction]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Microcontroller ATmega or PIC]
    J --> K[LCD Display SBP DBP MAP and Pulse Rate]
    J --> L[Deflation Valve Control]
```

**Explanation:** The cuff is inflated via a DC pump. The MPX5050 pressure sensor measures cuff pressure. A separate channel extracts the small oscillation amplitude (pulsatile). The algorithm identifies MAP at maximum oscillation, then derives SBP and DBP using empirical coefficients (typically 0.5 × MAP and 0.8 × MAP).

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Electronic Stethoscope System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Body Surface Chest or Back] --> B[Piezoelectric or Electret Microphone Sensor]
    B --> C[Preamplifier Gain 100 INA128]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 1.5 kHz]
    D --> E[Notch Filter 50 Hz Mains Removal]
    E --> F1[Audio Path to Power Amplifier LM386]
    E --> F2[ADC to Microcontroller for PCG Display]
    F1 --> G[Headphone or Speaker Output]
    F2 --> H[DSP Spectrogram Generator FFT]
    H --> I[LCD Display Time Frequency PCG]
```

**Explanation:** Acoustic vibrations are transduced by a piezoelectric/electret sensor. The weak signal is amplified (×100), filtered to the heart/lung band, notch-filtered for 50 Hz hum, and then split into audio playback and digital FFT spectrogram for visual analysis.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Complete Multi-Parameter Patient Monitor (Integrated)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sensor_Module [Patient Sensor Module]
        A1[PPG Sensor SpO2 and HR]
        A2[Temperature Sensor NTC]
        A3[BP Cuff and Pressure Sensor]
        A4[Electronic Stethoscope]
    end
    subgraph Signal_Conditioning [Signal Conditioning Stage]
        B1[Amplifier and Filter for PPG]
        B2[Bridge and Amplifier for Temp]
        B3[Differential Amp for BP Sensor]
        B4[Preamplifier and Filter for Stethoscope]
    end
    subgraph Processing [Digital Processing Unit]
        C1[Microcontroller STM32 or Arduino]
        C2[ADC Multiplexed Inputs]
        C3[Algorithm Processing HR SpO2 BP Temp PCG]
    end
    subgraph Output [Output and Display]
        D1[LCD Display Real Time Values]
        D2[Buzzer Alarms Threshold]
        D3[Serial UART to PC for Logging]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> C2
    A2 --> B2 --> C2
    A3 --> B3 --> C2
    A4 --> B4 --> C2
    C2 --> C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
```

**Explanation:** This integrated block diagram represents a **clinical-grade multi-parameter monitor** as found in ICUs. It demonstrates how PPG, temperature, BP, and stethoscope sensors are multiplexed, conditioned, processed by a single microcontroller, and presented on a unified display with alarm functionality — a **core KTU lab experiment outcome**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Photoplethysmography (PPG). Mention its two main components in the output signal.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a non-invasive optical technique for measuring volumetric changes in peripheral blood flow using a light source (LED) and a photodetector.

The PPG signal consists of:
1. **AC component** (pulsatile, ~0.5–10 Hz): corresponds to arterial blood volume changes during each cardiac cycle.
2. **DC component** (non-pulsatile): corresponds to skin, bone, venous blood, and average tissue absorption.

The **AC/DC ratio** is the **Perfusion Index (PI)**, an indicator of peripheral perfusion quality.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for AC/DC explanation + 1 mark for PI.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is SpO₂? Why is the ratio-of-ratios technique used instead of a single wavelength?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**SpO₂ (Peripheral Oxygen Saturation)** is the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen in peripheral blood:
$$
SpO_2 = \frac{HbO_2}{HbO_2 + Hb} \times 100\%
$$

A single wavelength is insufficient because absorption depends on **skin color, tissue thickness, and bone density**, which vary between patients.

The **ratio-of-ratios** technique uses **two wavelengths (Red 660 nm and IR 940 nm)** and takes:
$$
R = \frac{(AC/DC)_{Red}}{(AC/DC)_{IR}}
$$
This **cancels out** tissue and bone effects (since they affect both wavelengths similarly), leaving only the differential absorption of HbO₂ vs. Hb.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark single-wavelength limitation + 1 mark ratio-of-ratios justification.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Pulse Oximeter** to measure SpO₂ and heart rate. Discuss the role of red and infrared LEDs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pulse oximeter measures the following values: $M_R = 0.04$, $M_{IR} = 0.08$. Given the empirical relation $SpO_2 = 110 - 25R$, calculate the SpO₂ and comment on the clinical status. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Pulse Oximeter — Block Diagram and Working (7 Marks):**

A pulse oximeter consists of the following blocks:

1. **Dual-wavelength LED driver:** Alternately switches a Red LED (660 nm) and an IR LED (940 nm). *(1 mark)*
2. **Probe (Fingertip/Earlobe clip):** Contains LEDs on one side and a photodiode on the opposite side (transmission mode) or adjacent (reflectance mode). *(1 mark)*
3. **Photodetector + Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):** Converts the photocurrent into a voltage proportional to transmitted light intensity. *(1 mark)*
4. **Bandpass Filter (0.5–10 Hz):** Isolates the pulsatile AC component from the DC baseline. *(1 mark)*
5. **AC/DC Separator:** Computes AC and DC components for both Red and IR channels. *(1 mark)*
6. **Ratio Calculator + Lookup Table:** Computes $R = (AC_R/DC_R) / (AC_{IR}/DC_{IR})$ and maps to SpO₂ using pre-calibrated data. *(1 mark)*
7. **Display and Alarm:** Microcontroller displays SpO₂, pulse rate, and triggers alarms for abnormal values. *(1 mark)*

**Role of Red and IR LEDs:**
- **HbO₂ absorbs more IR (940 nm)** and less Red (660 nm).
- **Hb absorbs more Red (660 nm)** and less IR (940 nm).
- This differential absorption is the **physical basis** of SpO₂ measurement.
- The use of two wavelengths allows cancellation of non-blood tissue effects.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio R:**

$$
R = \frac{M_R}{M_{IR}} = \frac{0.04}{0.08} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical equation:**

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 25R = 110 - 25(0.5)
$$

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 12.5 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Clinical Interpretation:**

- Normal SpO₂ range: **95% – 100%**.
- Measured SpO₂: **97.5%** → **NORMAL / HEALTHY**.
- The patient has **adequate oxygenation**; no respiratory intervention required.
- For complete assessment, also evaluate heart rate and perfusion index.

*(2 marks for ratio computation + 2 marks for SpO₂ calculation + 2 marks for clinical interpretation + 1 mark for unit/comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the working of an **electronic stethoscope** with a block diagram. Compare its advantages with a conventional acoustic stethoscope. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has β = 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at body temperature (37 °C). Given a Wheatstone bridge with three fixed resistors of 10 kΩ, find the bridge output voltage at 37 °C if the excitation is 5 V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Electronic Stethoscope (7 Marks):**

**Working Principle:** Body sounds (heart/lung) are acoustic vibrations that are converted into electrical signals by a sensor, processed electronically, and presented as amplified audio or visual spectrograms.

**Block Diagram and Stages:**

1. **Acoustic Sensor:** Electret condenser microphone or piezoelectric diaphragm (sensitivity ~5 mV/Pa). *(1 mark)*
2. **Preamplifier:** Instrumentation amplifier (INA128) with gain ~100 to lift signal from mV to V range. *(1 mark)*
3. **Bandpass Filter:** 20 Hz – 1.5 kHz for heart sounds; 100 Hz – 1.5 kHz for lung sounds. *(1 mark)*
4. **50 Hz Notch Filter:** Removes mains power line interference. *(1 mark)*
5. **Power Amplifier:** LM386 with variable gain (20–200) to drive a speaker/headphone. *(1 mark)*
6. **DSP / Display (optional):** FFT-based spectrogram (PCG) for visual analysis; Bluetooth/USB output. *(1 mark)*
7. **Comparison with conventional stethoscope:** *(1 mark for table)*

| Feature | Acoustic Stethoscope | Electronic Stethoscope |
|---|---|---|
| Amplification | None | 20–200× |
| Frequency response | Limited (~100 Hz – 1 kHz) | Wide (20 Hz – 1.5 kHz) |
| Recording | Not possible | Possible (audio + spectrogram) |
| Noise immunity | Low | High (filtering) |
| Telemedicine | No | Yes |
| Power needed | No | Yes (battery) |
| Cost | Low | High |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Reference values:**

- $R_0 = 10\ k\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\ ^\circ C = 298.15\ K$
- $\beta = 3950\ K$
- $T = 37\ ^\circ C = 310.15\ K$

**Step 2 — Apply the β-parameter equation:**

$$
R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}
$$

$$
R_T = 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \left(\frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{310.15} = 3.2243 \times 10^{-3}\ K^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}\ K^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta = 3.2243 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -1.297 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}
$$

$$
\beta \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-1.297 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.5123
$$

$$
R_T = 10000 \times e^{-0.5123} = 10000 \times 0.5990 = 5990\ \Omega \approx 5.99\ k\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output voltage:**

For a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms at balance ($R_T = 5.99\ k\Omega$, other three arms = 10 kΩ):

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\frac{R_T}{R_T + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3}\right)
$$

With $R_2 = R_3 = 10\ k\Omega$ and $R_1 = 10\ k\Omega$:

$$
V_{out} = 5 \left(\frac{5990}{5990 + 10000} - \frac{10000}{10000 + 10000}\right)
$$

$$
= 5 \left(0.3747 - 0.5\right) = 5 \times (-0.1253) = -0.6265\ V
$$

**Magnitude of output:** $|V_{out}| = 0.627\ V$ (negative sign indicates bridge direction).

*(1 mark for formula + 2 marks for substitution + 2 marks for R_T calculation + 2 marks for V_out calculation. Always include units.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Missing Units:** "SpO₂ = 97.5" is incomplete. Always write "97.5%". *(−0.5 to −1 mark)*
> 2. **Skipping Block Diagram:** In a 14-mark question, a block diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect −3 to −4 marks.
